[{"content":"You are a report card writer for a school teacher. The student notes are the sole source of facts for the report. Every observation, data field, and mark must come from the notes — not from any examples. ## Style & Layout Guide The following are example report cards. Match their tone, voice, vocabulary, section headings, and layout exactly. These examples may differ slightly from the provided additional instructions - in that case, the instructions from the user take precedence. IMPORTANT: Do not copy specific Data field values, Marks, or observations from the examples — only include a Data field or Marks section if that value is present in the student notes. ### Example 1: Informes 3r trimestre Camille Martin null.pdf Kids&Us. Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term --- Term report (3rd term) Data Name: Camille Martin Teacher: Christine Blois Level: Emma 2024/2025 Absences: 2 Frequency of use (audio): Insufficient Participation/Motivation Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good Comment: Camille comes to class in a good mood and a smile on her face. She participates enthusiastically in all the activities. Camille truly loves story time. She likes reciting the story with her classmates and her teacher. Camille also really enjoys the "Numbers song", the "Can you please...?", the "Happy face/sad face card game" and the "Where is Emma's tattoo?" activities. Camille loves singing along to the songs and she also shows a real interest in the worksheet activities. Camille is always cooperative with her teacher and her classmates. Learning process Assessment (Learning process): Good Comment: Camille's English knowledge is good. She knows a great amount of vocabulary and structures. She can often use them in sentences, sometimes requiring the teacher's help to do so. This term, she specifically learnt the different actions and the different rooms/places in which those actions take place (e.g.: sleep in the bedroom, eat in the kitchen, etc.). She has also reinforced her knowledge of the shapes, the colours, the moods, the body parts and the food items. Behaviour Assessment (Behaviour): Very good Comment: Camille is a joyful, attentive, friendly and bright student. She treats her classmates and her teacher with respect and care. She listens attentively in class and usually stays focused during the lesson, seldom being distracted by her classmates. On a few occasions, she jokingly kept the material instead of giving it back to the teacher, but usually, gives it back directly when asked. Moreover, she takes good care of the material and always shares it with her classmates. Marks Oral Test Mark: 7.20 Good. Camille can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct. The decrease in her audio listening frequency might explain her good result, instead of a very good one! The teacher still wants to congratulate Camille on her good result! Keep up the good work! --- Final comment At the beginning of the school year, Camille was a very quiet and shy student. Thanks to her classmates’ friendliness and her easy-going attitude, she came out of her shell and has felt comfortable in the class ever since. It has truly been a joy to have Camille in class, thanks to her smiling and her good-humoured attitude. Furthermore, she has also made progress in her English learning, developing both her memory and her comprehension skills. It is recommended to listen to the audio tracks during the summer holidays, in order to keep in mind the vocabulary and structures, learnt throughout the school year. ### Example 2: Informes 3r trimestre Luis Moreau Very Good Emma.pdf Kids&Us. Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term --- Term report (3rd term) Data Name: Luis Moreau Teacher: Christine Blois Level: Emma 2024/2025 Absences: 0 Frequency of use (audio): Pass Participation/Motivation Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good Comment: Luis usually comes to class in a good and calm mood. He usually participates in the activities with a certain enthusiasm. Luis really likes story time and he takes an active role in the storytelling, reciting the story with the teacher. He also seemed to really enjoy the "Can you please...?", the "Happy face/sad face card game", the "Where can Emma...?" and the "Where can you...?" activities. Luis is always cooperative with his classmates and his teacher. Learning process Assessment (Learning process): Very good Comment: Luis' English knowledge is very good. He knows a great amount of vocabulary and structures. He can often use them in sentences, sometimes requiring the teacher's help to do so. This term, he specifically learnt the different actions and the different rooms/places in which those actions take place (e.g. sleep in the bedroom, eat in the kitchen, etc.). He has also reinforced his knowledge of the shapes, the colours, the moods, the body parts and the food items. Behaviour Assessment (Behaviour): Very good Comment: Luis is a composed, gentle, friendly and attentive student. He always treats his classmates and his teacher with respect and care. He is seldom distracted by his classmates or external elements. He usually stays focused and listens attentively, following the teacher's instructions very well. Luis, always treats the material with care, gives the material back directly when asked, and shares it with his classmates. Marks Oral Test Mark: 8.40 Very good. Luis can understand the question and makes an effort to use a complete structure in the answer, even if it is not always the correct one. Congratulations on your very good result, Luis! We're very proud of you! --- Final comment Throughout the year, Luis has opened up and has seemed to really feel more comfortable with his classmates. It is a real joy to have Luis in class, thanks to his diligent and mindful attitude, as well as his friendliness. Moreover, Luis kept developing both his memory and comprehension skills. His oral test results have been in constant progress. Well done, Luis! Furthermore, in order to keep in mind the vocabulary and structures learnt throughout the school year, it is recommended to listen to the audio track during the summer holidays. ### Example 3: Informes 3r trimestre Rayan Bernard de Fortemps Acceptable Emma.pdf Kids&Us. Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term --- Term report (3rd term) Data Name: Rayan Bernard Teacher: Christine Blois Level: Emma 2024/2025 Absences: 0 Frequency of use (audio): Insufficient Participation/Motivation Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good Comment: Rayan usually comes to class in a cheerful and lively mood. He participates in the activities with great enthusiasm. Rayan does not seem overly interested in story time, usually losing focus during that activity. However, he really enjoyed the "Can you please...?", the "Happy face/sad face card game", the "Where is Emma's tattoo?", the "Where can Emma...?" and the "Where can you...?" activities. Rayan is usually quite cooperative with his classmates and his teacher. Learning process Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory Comment: Rayan's English knowledge is satisfactory. He does know a certain amount of vocabulary and structures. However, he usually requires the teacher's help to repeat the full structures and tell him how he must use words in sentences. This term he specifically learnt the different actions and the different rooms/places in which those actions take place (e.g.: sleep in the bedroom, eat in the kitchen, etc.). He has also reinforced his knowledge of the shapes, the colours, the moods, the body parts and the food items. Behaviour Assessment (Behaviour): Good Comment: Rayan is a fun-loving, energetic and good-humoured student. He seldom treats his classmates in an unfriendly manner, generally treating them with respect and care. He also generally treats his teacher in a respectful manner. Rayan can still, at times, be distracted by external elements and his classmates. However, he has learnt to stay more focused and listen more attentively to the teacher, following the teacher's instructions rather well. He tends to treat the material with care and can give it back directly when asked, and usually shares it with his classmates. Marks Oral Test Mark: 6.20 Acceptable. Rayan can understand the question but, in most cases, still does not respond with a complete structure. The teacher had noticed a real improvement in Rayan's learning, when Rayan's audio listening frequency was more regular. He was able to understand what was expected of him during the activities and the meaning of the words used in the activities. Keep up the good work, Rayan! --- Final comment Throughout the school year and especially this term, Rayan’s behaviour has really improved. He still needs to move around and explore his surroundings, but he does so in a calmer way. He has been getting better at listening to the teacher and staying more focused. He has kept his grades up and with some work and a regular audio listening, his English level can only increase! It is recommended to listen to the audio tracks during the summer holidays, in order to keep in mind the vocabulary and structures learnt throughout the year. ## Important Additional Instructions from User - Takes precedence over any provided example MOTIVATION AND PARTICIPATION: o Motivation: definition: factor or group of factors that bring about a specific type of behaviour. This section should comment on whether the student shows a positive attitude towards different activities conducted in the classroom: ▪ How does the student come into the classroom? ▪ Do they show willingness to learn? ▪ Are they open to doing new activities? ▪ Are they cooperative with the rest of the group and with the teacher? o Participation: definition: involvement in an event, act or activity. Parents must be informed whether their child participates in the activities that are carried out in the classroom and whether or not they put previously-acquired knowledge, as well as language currently being worked on, into practice. ▪ Outline the degree of participation based on what the student views as being important. For example, he/she is very competitive and always wants to win. He/she gets cross when they don't win. He/she always wants to come first. ▪ Another noteworthy aspect is whether the student holds back when new material is introduced, and whether they take part to a lesser degree than when internalised knowledge is elicited in class. BEHAVIOUR: definition: the way in which a person behaves in a specific or general situation. o Note whether the student is integrated within the group or whether they are easily distracted or unable to focus. o Point out, if necessary, how a student is reacting in light of situations that have had to be tackled with a specific plan. o Explain whether the environment or general mood of the group, or the student's particular mood, affects the student's behaviour. Is the group more easily distracted than normal? Is the student especially tired on rainy days, after public holidays, etc.? 14 LEARNING: definition: the process of studying, practising or experiencing whereby an individual or a group acquires specific knowledge, particularly knowledge required for learning an art form or profession. o Use of structures worked on during the story o Level of learning objectives achieved o Strengths o Weaknesses, together with a plan for improvement and commitment on behalf of the school to work on this aspect. Avoid repetitions in the sections ## Student Notes (source of truth — all report content must derive from these) Student: Chloé, Class: Emma Saturday - 2026-05-14: Chloé did not know the colours or the clothes. The Emma Saturday students were fun. They behaved quite well and sometimes needed to be reminded that they could be in orange instead of green, and it worked wonders. Léo knew or at least understood the worksheet activity very well. They all could understand the clean and dirty concept and dry and wet too. Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Chloé
Teacher: [Not provided in notes]
Level: Emma Saturday
Absences: [Not provided in notes]

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Chloé usually comes to class in a good mood. She participates enthusiastically in all the activities. Chloé enjoys story time and can repeat the story with the classmates and the teacher. She also shows a real interest in the worksheet activities and the "what's missing" game. Chloé can tell her age and gender, often using the complete structure. She can pair up Emma’s dirty/clean body parts and place tattoos on the correct body parts with a little help. Chloé can also pair up the correct actions in the correct rooms and repeat new vocabulary words and structures. She is usually cooperative with her teacher and her classmates.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Chloé’s English knowledge is satisfactory. She knows some vocabulary and structures but sometimes needs the teacher’s help to repeat full structures and use words in sentences. This term, she specifically learnt the different actions and the different rooms/places in which those actions take place. She has also reinforced her knowledge of the body parts, the clothes items, and the concepts of clean/dirty and dry/wet. Chloé can count well and understand the corresponding colour and clothing item on her bingo card. She can put clothes items on and take them off, and with help, she can put the correct coloured items. Chloé can pair up the correct actions in the correct rooms and repeat new vocabulary words and structures.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Chloé is a friendly and energetic student. She treats her classmates and her teacher with respect and care. She listens attentively in class and usually stays focused during the lesson, though she can be distracted at times. Chloé has learnt to stay more focused and listen more attentively to the teacher, following instructions rather well. She takes good care of the material and usually shares it with her classmates. On a few occasions, she asked repetitive questions, which could be distracting, but she stopped when reminded.

Final comment

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown enthusiasm and engagement in the classroom activities. She has made progress in her English learning, developing her vocabulary and comprehension skills. Chloé’s willingness to participate and her cooperative attitude have contributed to a positive learning environment. It is recommended to continue practising the vocabulary and structures learnt this term to reinforce her progress.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

---

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

Chloé generally comes to class in a cheerful mood and participates in activities with enthusiasm. She enjoys activities like the new story tattoo and can tell her age and gender using complete structures. However, she sometimes gets distracted and asks repetitive questions ("quoi quoi quoi"), which can disrupt the class. She is generally cooperative in group activities, though her repetitive questioning can be disruptive.

---

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:

Chloé's English knowledge has improved throughout the term. Initially, she did not know colors or clothes vocabulary, but she has since reinforced her knowledge of these areas. She can tell her age and gender using complete structures and can pair dirty/clean body parts with reminders for full structures. She performs well in worksheet activities and enjoys the "what's missing" task. She has also learned to pair correct actions with their corresponding rooms and can repeat new vocabulary and structures with some assistance.

---

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Chloé's behavior is generally good, but she can be easily distracted and sometimes loud, requiring reminders to stay focused. She has shown improvement in calming down when reminded and participates well in group activities. She generally treats her classmates and teacher with respect and is cooperative in group settings. On occasion, her repetitive questioning can be disruptive, but she responds well to gentle corrections.

---

Final comment

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown steady progress in her English learning. She has improved her vocabulary and ability to use structures in sentences, particularly in areas like colors, clothes, and body parts. While she sometimes struggles with focus and asks repetitive questions, she responds well to gentle reminders and is cooperative in group settings. Her enthusiasm for activities like the new story tattoo and worksheet tasks demonstrates a positive attitude towards learning. With continued practice and reinforcement of listening skills, Chloé is well on her way to further improving her language abilities.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Teacher: [Teacher]
  • Level: Emma Saturday 2025/2026

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Chloé usually comes to class in a good mood and is generally willing to take part in the activities proposed. She is open to new tasks and, once reminded of the classroom routine, she joins in with enthusiasm. At the beginning of some sessions, she could be a little loud or repeatedly ask for confirmation, but she usually settles well when given clear guidance. Chloé is cooperative with the teacher and with her classmates, and she especially enjoys hands-on activities such as worksheet work, bingo, cards and dressing Emma.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:
Chloé's English knowledge is satisfactory. She can repeat the new structures well and often understands the language being worked on in class. This term, she worked on colours, clothes, body parts, actions, rooms and places, as well as the concepts of clean/dirty and dry/wet. She can usually tell her age and gender and, on several occasions, used the complete structure correctly. She was also able to pair actions with the correct rooms and take part in the story-time activities. However, she still needs more practice with some vocabulary items, especially clothes and colours, and she sometimes needs the teacher's help to remember or apply the full structure. With more regular revision, she will continue to gain confidence and accuracy.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Chloé is a lively and generally well-integrated student. She can be a little distracted or impatient at times, especially when she is tired or when the group is especially active, but she responds well to reminders and class management strategies. Once the rules are clear, she behaves appropriately and stays focused on the task. She usually treats the material carefully and works well with the rest of the group. Her attitude in class is positive and she is able to adapt well to the pace of the lesson.

Final comment

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown a positive attitude towards the classroom routines and has taken part well in the activities. She is able to understand and repeat a good part of the language worked on in class, and she is becoming more confident when using complete structures. She still needs to keep reinforcing some vocabulary, especially clothes and colours, but she is making good progress. Well done, Chloé! It is recommended to keep listening to the audio tracks and revising the vocabulary at home in order to consolidate the language learnt during the term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus a Final comment, as shown in the reference. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The report should synthesise observations from multiple notes into a cohesive narrative. IMPORTANT: Do not invent Data fields (Absences, Frequency of use, Teacher name, Oral Test Mark, or other numerical/structured metadata) — only include facts traceable to the source notes. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations, no fabricated marks, absences counts, or audio-frequency values) - synthesis: observations from multiple notes woven together, not a note-by-note list - tone: warm, encouraging, matches Emma-level examples "}]

response.output:
```html

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

---

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

Chloé generally comes to class in a cheerful mood and participates in activities with enthusiasm. She enjoys activities like the new story tattoo and can tell her age and gender using complete structures. However, she sometimes gets distracted and asks repetitive questions ("quoi quoi quoi"), which can disrupt the class. She is generally cooperative in group activities, though her repetitive questioning can be disruptive.

---

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:

Chloé's English knowledge has improved throughout the term. Initially, she did not know colors or clothes vocabulary, but she has since reinforced her knowledge of these areas. She can tell her age and gender using complete structures and can pair dirty/clean body parts with reminders for full structures. She performs well in worksheet activities and enjoys the "what's missing" task. She has also learned to pair correct actions with their corresponding rooms and can repeat new vocabulary and structures with some assistance.

---

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Chloé's behavior is generally good, but she can be easily distracted and sometimes loud, requiring reminders to stay focused. She has shown improvement in calming down when reminded and participates well in group activities. She generally treats her classmates and teacher with respect and is cooperative in group settings. On occasion, her repetitive questioning can be disruptive, but she responds well to gentle corrections.

---

Final comment

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown steady progress in her English learning. She has improved her vocabulary and ability to use structures in sentences, particularly in areas like colors, clothes, and body parts. While she sometimes struggles with focus and asks repetitive questions, she responds well to gentle reminders and is cooperative in group settings. Her enthusiasm for activities like the new story tattoo and worksheet tasks demonstrates a positive attitude towards learning. With continued practice and reinforcement of listening skills, Chloé is well on her way to further improving her language abilities.

```

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Class: Emma Saturday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

Chloé usually comes to class in a good mood, though she can sometimes be distracted or tired. She shows willingness to participate in activities, especially when they involve repetition or familiar structures. Chloé enjoys interactive tasks such as the "what's missing" activity and dressing Emma, where she engages well with the material. She is cooperative with her classmates and teacher, though she occasionally needs reminders to focus—particularly when asking repetitive questions about tasks she already understands. Her participation improves when the group is reminded of classroom expectations, such as using the traffic light system.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:

Chloé’s English knowledge is developing, though she still struggles with certain vocabulary areas. She has difficulty with colours and clothing items, often requiring teacher support to identify or pair them correctly. However, she demonstrates strength in repeating structures, such as those used in the "Emma is wearing..." activity, and can apply them with guidance. Chloé understands conceptual opposites like clean/dirty and dry/wet, and she can follow instructions for worksheets and counting tasks effectively. She knows how to state her age and gender using nearly complete structures. Her comprehension of the story improves with repetition, though she initially needed significant help to retell it. With practice, she has shown progress in pairing actions with rooms and repeating new vocabulary.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Chloé is a playful and generally well-behaved student who integrates well within her group. She can be loud or distracted at times, particularly at the start of class or when tired, but responds positively to reminders and the traffic light system. While she occasionally seeks excessive reassurance (e.g., repeatedly asking where to place items despite knowing the answer), she stops when redirected. Chloé treats her classmates and teacher respectfully and participates in group activities without disruption. Her focus improves when tasks are clear and structured, though she may need individual attention during new or complex activities, such as vocabulary card pairing.

---

Final comment

Chloé has made steady progress this term, particularly in her ability to follow routines and repeat structures. While she still needs support with specific vocabulary (colours, clothing) and occasionally requires reminders to stay on task, her cooperative attitude and improving participation are encouraging. Her comprehension of stories and conceptual opposites has strengthened with practice, and she engages well in hands-on activities like dressing Emma or worksheet tasks. To build on her progress, continued reinforcement of vocabulary—especially through repetitive, interactive exercises—would be beneficial. Chloé’s enthusiasm and willingness to participate, when focused, make her a valued member of the class. With consistent practice, her confidence in using English structures independently will continue to grow.

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Teacher: [Teacher]
  • Level: Emma Saturday 2025/2026

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Chloé usually comes to class in a good mood and is generally willing to take part in the activities proposed. She is open to new tasks and, once reminded of the classroom routine, she joins in with enthusiasm. At the beginning of some sessions, she could be a little loud or repeatedly ask for confirmation, but she usually settles well when given clear guidance. Chloé is cooperative with the teacher and with her classmates, and she especially enjoys hands-on activities such as worksheet work, bingo, cards and dressing Emma.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:
Chloé's English knowledge is satisfactory. She can repeat the new structures well and often understands the language being worked on in class. This term, she worked on colours, clothes, body parts, actions, rooms and places, as well as the concepts of clean/dirty and dry/wet. She can usually tell her age and gender and, on several occasions, used the complete structure correctly. She was also able to pair actions with the correct rooms and take part in the story-time activities. However, she still needs more practice with some vocabulary items, especially clothes and colours, and she sometimes needs the teacher's help to remember or apply the full structure. With more regular revision, she will continue to gain confidence and accuracy.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Chloé is a lively and generally well-integrated student. She can be a little distracted or impatient at times, especially when she is tired or when the group is especially active, but she responds well to reminders and class management strategies. Once the rules are clear, she behaves appropriately and stays focused on the task. She usually treats the material carefully and works well with the rest of the group. Her attitude in class is positive and she is able to adapt well to the pace of the lesson.

Final comment

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown a positive attitude towards the classroom routines and has taken part well in the activities. She is able to understand and repeat a good part of the language worked on in class, and she is becoming more confident when using complete structures. She still needs to keep reinforcing some vocabulary, especially clothes and colours, but she is making good progress. Well done, Chloé! It is recommended to keep listening to the audio tracks and revising the vocabulary at home in order to consolidate the language learnt during the term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus a Final comment, as shown in the reference. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The report should synthesise observations from multiple notes into a cohesive narrative. IMPORTANT: Do not invent Data fields (Absences, Frequency of use, Teacher name, Oral Test Mark, or other numerical/structured metadata) — only include facts traceable to the source notes. response.output:
```html

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Class: Emma Saturday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

Chloé usually comes to class in a good mood, though she can sometimes be distracted or tired. She shows willingness to participate in activities, especially when they involve repetition or familiar structures. Chloé enjoys interactive tasks such as the "what's missing" activity and dressing Emma, where she engages well with the material. She is cooperative with her classmates and teacher, though she occasionally needs reminders to focus—particularly when asking repetitive questions about tasks she already understands. Her participation improves when the group is reminded of classroom expectations, such as using the traffic light system.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:

Chloé’s English knowledge is developing, though she still struggles with certain vocabulary areas. She has difficulty with colours and clothing items, often requiring teacher support to identify or pair them correctly. However, she demonstrates strength in repeating structures, such as those used in the "Emma is wearing..." activity, and can apply them with guidance. Chloé understands conceptual opposites like clean/dirty and dry/wet, and she can follow instructions for worksheets and counting tasks effectively. She knows how to state her age and gender using nearly complete structures. Her comprehension of the story improves with repetition, though she initially needed significant help to retell it. With practice, she has shown progress in pairing actions with rooms and repeating new vocabulary.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Chloé is a playful and generally well-behaved student who integrates well within her group. She can be loud or distracted at times, particularly at the start of class or when tired, but responds positively to reminders and the traffic light system. While she occasionally seeks excessive reassurance (e.g., repeatedly asking where to place items despite knowing the answer), she stops when redirected. Chloé treats her classmates and teacher respectfully and participates in group activities without disruption. Her focus improves when tasks are clear and structured, though she may need individual attention during new or complex activities, such as vocabulary card pairing.

---

Final comment

Chloé has made steady progress this term, particularly in her ability to follow routines and repeat structures. While she still needs support with specific vocabulary (colours, clothing) and occasionally requires reminders to stay on task, her cooperative attitude and improving participation are encouraging. Her comprehension of stories and conceptual opposites has strengthened with practice, and she engages well in hands-on activities like dressing Emma or worksheet tasks. To build on her progress, continued reinforcement of vocabulary—especially through repetitive, interactive exercises—would be beneficial. Chloé’s enthusiasm and willingness to participate, when focused, make her a valued member of the class. With consistent practice, her confidence in using English structures independently will continue to grow.

```

Is the group more easily distracted than normal? Is the student especially tired on rainy days, after public holidays, etc.? 14 LEARNING: definition: the process of studying, practising or experiencing whereby an individual or a group acquires specific knowledge, particularly knowledge required for learning an art form or profession. o Use of structures worked on during the story o Level of learning objectives achieved o Strengths o Weaknesses, together with a plan for improvement and commitment on behalf of the school to work on this aspect. Avoid repetitions in the sections Reference report (a known-good output for the same notes — use as a quality benchmark, student name may differ):

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Teacher: [Teacher]
  • Level: Emma Saturday 2025/2026

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Chloé usually comes to class in a good mood and is generally willing to take part in the activities proposed. She is open to new tasks and, once reminded of the classroom routine, she joins in with enthusiasm. At the beginning of some sessions, she could be a little loud or repeatedly ask for confirmation, but she usually settles well when given clear guidance. Chloé is cooperative with the teacher and with her classmates, and she especially enjoys hands-on activities such as worksheet work, bingo, cards and dressing Emma.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:
Chloé's English knowledge is satisfactory. She can repeat the new structures well and often understands the language being worked on in class. This term, she worked on colours, clothes, body parts, actions, rooms and places, as well as the concepts of clean/dirty and dry/wet. She can usually tell her age and gender and, on several occasions, used the complete structure correctly. She was also able to pair actions with the correct rooms and take part in the story-time activities. However, she still needs more practice with some vocabulary items, especially clothes and colours, and she sometimes needs the teacher's help to remember or apply the full structure. With more regular revision, she will continue to gain confidence and accuracy.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Chloé is a lively and generally well-integrated student. She can be a little distracted or impatient at times, especially when she is tired or when the group is especially active, but she responds well to reminders and class management strategies. Once the rules are clear, she behaves appropriately and stays focused on the task. She usually treats the material carefully and works well with the rest of the group. Her attitude in class is positive and she is able to adapt well to the pace of the lesson.

Final comment

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown a positive attitude towards the classroom routines and has taken part well in the activities. She is able to understand and repeat a good part of the language worked on in class, and she is becoming more confident when using complete structures. She still needs to keep reinforcing some vocabulary, especially clothes and colours, but she is making good progress. Well done, Chloé! It is recommended to keep listening to the audio tracks and revising the vocabulary at home in order to consolidate the language learnt during the term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus a Final comment, as shown in the reference. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The report should synthesise observations from multiple notes into a cohesive narrative. IMPORTANT: Do not invent Data fields (Absences, Frequency of use, Teacher name, Oral Test Mark, or other numerical/structured metadata) — only include facts traceable to the source notes. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations, no fabricated marks, absences counts, or audio-frequency values) - synthesis: observations from multiple notes woven together, not a note-by-note list - tone: warm, encouraging, matches Emma-level examples

response.output:
Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Chloé
Teacher: [Not specified in notes]
Level: Emma Saturday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:
Chloé usually participates in class activities with enthusiasm, though her engagement varies. She enjoys the "What's missing" activity and the worksheet tasks, which she completes well. She is cooperative with her classmates and teacher, though she sometimes requires reminders to focus. Chloé shows willingness to learn, but at times she holds back, particularly when new material is introduced, such as the story or vocabulary cards, where she needed more guidance than her peers.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:
Chloé's English knowledge is developing. She can tell her age and gender using the complete structure and understands concepts like clean/dirty and dry/wet. She can pair actions with the correct rooms and repeat new vocabulary and structures. However, she struggles with colours and clothing vocabulary, often requiring teacher assistance to recall or use the correct terms. She partially understands the Numbers songs and needs reminders for full structures, such as in the "Emma is wearing..." activity. Her comprehension improves with repetition and support.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Chloé is generally well-behaved and integrates well with the group. She can be distracted and tired at times, which affects her focus, particularly during instructions. She occasionally asks repetitive questions, such as "quoi quoi quoi," but responds well to reminders. Chloé treats her classmates and teacher with respect and follows instructions when prompted. She is playful and energetic, aligning with the group's mood, which can be loud but calms down with reminders.

---

Final comment

Chloé has shown progress in her participation and understanding of English structures, particularly in activities she enjoys, like worksheets and interactive tasks. While she sometimes struggles with new vocabulary, such as colours and clothing, her ability to follow instructions and engage with her peers has improved. To continue her development, it would be beneficial for Chloé to focus on reinforcing vocabulary and structures she finds challenging. With consistent effort and support, she can build confidence in these areas.

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Chloé

Level: Emma Saturday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

Chloé usually comes to class in a very good mood. At the beginning of the lesson, the group was a bit loud and needed the traffic light, but after that they participated super well. Chloé liked the "what's missing" activity and was excited to hear more about the new story tattoo. When she needed to be reminded not to keep asking where things should go, she stopped.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:

Chloé's English knowledge is good. She can repeat the structures well and understands the "Emma is wearing..." activity. She could pair up the correct actions in the correct rooms, and she could also repeat the new vocabulary words and the new structures. She could tell her age and her gender, almost always using the complete structure, and she could pair up Emma's dirty and clean body parts. She did the worksheet activities very well and counted super well. She also understood the clean and dirty concept, as well as dry and wet. However, she did not know the colours or the clothes, did not know the story at all on one occasion and needed help to tell it, and she still needs support with the clothing items vocabulary. She partially understood the Numbers songs.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Chloé was in a very good mood. She was sometimes a bit loud at the beginning and could be distracted or tired, but she could calm down when reminded. She behaved quite well and liked the new story tattoo. She was excited to hear more, could place the tattoos on the right body part with a little help, and could also place stickers on her body parts.

Final comment

Chloé participated super well once the traffic light was in place, and she was able to repeat the new vocabulary words and structures. She could tell her age and her gender, and she did the worksheet activities and counting very well. She still needs support with the story, the colours and the clothing vocabulary, but she responds well when reminded.

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Teacher: [Teacher]
  • Level: Emma Saturday 2025/2026

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Chloé usually comes to class in a good mood and is generally willing to take part in the activities proposed. She is open to new tasks and, once reminded of the classroom routine, she joins in with enthusiasm. At the beginning of some sessions, she could be a little loud or repeatedly ask for confirmation, but she usually settles well when given clear guidance. Chloé is cooperative with the teacher and with her classmates, and she especially enjoys hands-on activities such as worksheet work, bingo, cards and dressing Emma.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:
Chloé's English knowledge is satisfactory. She can repeat the new structures well and often understands the language being worked on in class. This term, she worked on colours, clothes, body parts, actions, rooms and places, as well as the concepts of clean/dirty and dry/wet. She can usually tell her age and gender and, on several occasions, used the complete structure correctly. She was also able to pair actions with the correct rooms and take part in the story-time activities. However, she still needs more practice with some vocabulary items, especially clothes and colours, and she sometimes needs the teacher's help to remember or apply the full structure. With more regular revision, she will continue to gain confidence and accuracy.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Chloé is a lively and generally well-integrated student. She can be a little distracted or impatient at times, especially when she is tired or when the group is especially active, but she responds well to reminders and class management strategies. Once the rules are clear, she behaves appropriately and stays focused on the task. She usually treats the material carefully and works well with the rest of the group. Her attitude in class is positive and she is able to adapt well to the pace of the lesson.

Final comment

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown a positive attitude towards the classroom routines and has taken part well in the activities. She is able to understand and repeat a good part of the language worked on in class, and she is becoming more confident when using complete structures. She still needs to keep reinforcing some vocabulary, especially clothes and colours, but she is making good progress. Well done, Chloé! It is recommended to keep listening to the audio tracks and revising the vocabulary at home in order to consolidate the language learnt during the term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus a Final comment, as shown in the reference. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The report should synthesise observations from multiple notes into a cohesive narrative. Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Teacher: [Teacher]
  • Level: Emma Saturday 2025/2026

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Chloé usually comes to class in a good mood and is generally willing to take part in the activities proposed. She is open to new tasks and, once reminded of the classroom routine, she joins in with enthusiasm. At the beginning of some sessions, she could be a little loud or repeatedly ask for confirmation, but she usually settles well when given clear guidance. Chloé is cooperative with the teacher and with her classmates, and she especially enjoys hands-on activities such as worksheet work, bingo, cards and dressing Emma.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:
Chloé's English knowledge is satisfactory. She can repeat the new structures well and often understands the language being worked on in class. This term, she worked on colours, clothes, body parts, actions, rooms and places, as well as the concepts of clean/dirty and dry/wet. She can usually tell her age and gender and, on several occasions, used the complete structure correctly. She was also able to pair actions with the correct rooms and take part in the story-time activities. However, she still needs more practice with some vocabulary items, especially clothes and colours, and she sometimes needs the teacher's help to remember or apply the full structure. With more regular revision, she will continue to gain confidence and accuracy.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Chloé is a lively and generally well-integrated student. She can be a little distracted or impatient at times, especially when she is tired or when the group is especially active, but she responds well to reminders and class management strategies. Once the rules are clear, she behaves appropriately and stays focused on the task. She usually treats the material carefully and works well with the rest of the group. Her attitude in class is positive and she is able to adapt well to the pace of the lesson.

Final comment

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown a positive attitude towards the classroom routines and has taken part well in the activities. She is able to understand and repeat a good part of the language worked on in class, and she is becoming more confident when using complete structures. She still needs to keep reinforcing some vocabulary, especially clothes and colours, but she is making good progress. Well done, Chloé! It is recommended to keep listening to the audio tracks and revising the vocabulary at home in order to consolidate the language learnt during the term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus a Final comment, as shown in the reference. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The report should synthesise observations from multiple notes into a cohesive narrative. IMPORTANT: Do not invent Data fields (Absences, Frequency of use, Teacher name, Oral Test Mark, or other numerical/structured metadata) — only include facts traceable to the source notes. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations, no fabricated marks, absences counts, or audio-frequency values) - synthesis: observations from multiple notes woven together, not a note-by-note list - tone: warm, encouraging, matches Emma-level examples

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Chloé

Level: Emma Saturday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

Chloé usually comes to class in a very good mood. At the beginning of the lesson, the group was a bit loud and needed the traffic light, but after that they participated super well. Chloé liked the "what's missing" activity and was excited to hear more about the new story tattoo. When she needed to be reminded not to keep asking where things should go, she stopped.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:

Chloé's English knowledge is good. She can repeat the structures well and understands the "Emma is wearing..." activity. She could pair up the correct actions in the correct rooms, and she could also repeat the new vocabulary words and the new structures. She could tell her age and her gender, almost always using the complete structure, and she could pair up Emma's dirty and clean body parts. She did the worksheet activities very well and counted super well. She also understood the clean and dirty concept, as well as dry and wet. However, she did not know the colours or the clothes, did not know the story at all on one occasion and needed help to tell it, and she still needs support with the clothing items vocabulary. She partially understood the Numbers songs.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Chloé was in a very good mood. She was sometimes a bit loud at the beginning and could be distracted or tired, but she could calm down when reminded. She behaved quite well and liked the new story tattoo. She was excited to hear more, could place the tattoos on the right body part with a little help, and could also place stickers on her body parts.

Final comment

Chloé participated super well once the traffic light was in place, and she was able to repeat the new vocabulary words and structures. She could tell her age and her gender, and she did the worksheet activities and counting very well. She still needs support with the story, the colours and the clothing vocabulary, but she responds well when reminded.

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Mila
  • Class: Oliver Thursday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Satisfactory

Comment:
Mila shows a generally positive attitude in class, particularly when activities align with her interests. She eagerly participates in hands-on tasks like the Dice Game, Family Bingo, and A Present for Me, where she demonstrates enthusiasm and a willingness to engage. Her motivation is strongest when she feels confident, such as when retelling stories or using familiar vocabulary and structures. However, Mila occasionally withdraws when faced with challenges or when things do not go her way—whether it’s not receiving the stamp, not being allowed to keep the blu-tack, or not having a clear view of the Big Book. These moments can lead to frustration, causing her to disengage temporarily, such as refusing to participate in the True or False activity or turning away during story time. Encouraging her resilience and reinforcing her many strengths—like her ability to combine moods and actions or her quick grasp of new vocabulary—will help her stay motivated even during difficult tasks.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:
Mila has made notable progress in her language acquisition, particularly in vocabulary and story retelling. She demonstrates a strong understanding of the Oliver story, often recounting it with minimal support, and excels in activities involving weather routines, clothing items, and color vocabulary (such as identifying white and black). Her ability to pair weather realia items with correct sentences and use structures like "putting on" and "taking off" (with occasional guidance) reflects her growing confidence. However, Mila sometimes struggles with patience during longer activities, as seen when the class grew restless by the end of the story. To build on her strengths, she should continue practising less internalised vocabulary, such as the words for "taking off" and "putting on," and work on maintaining focus during multi-step tasks. Her quick adaptation to new material, like describing what she’s wearing, is commendable and should be further nurtured.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Satisfactory

Comment:
Mila’s behaviour in class is generally cooperative, and she interacts well with her peers, especially during group activities like handing out materials. She shows moments of excellent participation, such as when she combined moods and actions effectively or completed worksheet tasks with ease. However, her mood can fluctuate—she may lay down, turn away, or express frustration (e.g., claiming a headache) when faced with minor setbacks or unmet expectations. These reactions sometimes disrupt her focus, as seen when she refused to engage in the True or False game or during story time. With gentle reminders and positive reinforcement, Mila quickly recovers her enthusiasm, as demonstrated when she rejoined activities after initial hesitation. Helping her develop strategies to manage frustration—such as asking for help or adjusting her position to see the Big Book—will support her in maintaining a consistent, positive presence in the classroom.

Final comment

________________________________________________________________________________
Mila has shown a mix of impressive strengths and areas for growth this term. Her ability to retell stories, use new vocabulary, and engage in interactive games highlights her potential and enthusiasm for learning. While she occasionally struggles with emotional regulation when faced with challenges, her willingness to re-engage—once supported—demonstrates her resilience. Continued practice with patience and focus, along with celebrating her many successes (like her mastery of color words and story structures), will help her build confidence and consistency. With her bright and participative spirit, Mila is well on her way to further progress in her language journey.

Parents must be informed whether their child participates in the activities that are carried out in the classroom and whether or not they put previously-acquired knowledge, as well as language currently being worked on, into practice. ▪ Outline the degree of participation based on what the student views as being important. He/she always wants to come first. ▪ Another noteworthy aspect is whether the student holds back when new material is introduced, and whether they take part to a lesser degree than when internalised knowledge is elicited in class. BEHAVIOUR: definition: the way in which a person behaves in a specific or general situation. o Note whether the student is integrated within the group or whether they are easily distracted or unable to focus. o Point out, if necessary, how a student is reacting in light of situations that have had to be tackled with a specific plan. o Explain whether the environment or general mood of the group, or the student's particular mood, affects the student's behaviour. Is the group more easily distracted than normal? Is the student especially tired on rainy days, after public holidays, etc.? 14 LEARNING: definition: the process of studying, practising or experiencing whereby an individual or a group acquires specific knowledge, particularly knowledge required for learning an art form or profession. o Use of structures worked on during the story o Level of learning objectives achieved o Strengths o Weaknesses, together with a plan for improvement and commitment on behalf of the school to work on this aspect. Avoid repetitions in the same section. Keep an encouraging and positive tone Reference report (a known-good output for the same notes — use as a quality benchmark, student name may differ):

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Mila
  • Teacher: Thérèse Garnier
  • Level: Oliver Thursday 2025/2026
  • Period: 2026-02-22 to 2026-05-22

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment: Mila generally comes to class in a positive mood and shows interest in the activities presented. She is usually willing to participate and can be very engaged when she feels comfortable with the task, especially in action-based activities and games. At times, however, she can become upset when things do not go as she expects, which affects her willingness to continue taking part. With encouragement, she is able to rejoin the class and show good motivation, particularly when she has the opportunity to help or demonstrate what she knows.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment: Mila has shown good understanding of the structures and vocabulary worked on this term. She can repeat the target language well and, in many activities, uses the new language with confidence. Her knowledge of the story has improved notably, and she is able to retell parts of it with little support. She also performs very well in activities involving weather, clothing, and colour vocabulary. At times, she still needs help with some specific words and routines, such as taking off and putting on, and she may need further practice to remain consistent when new language is introduced. Continued repetition and reinforcement will help her consolidate her progress even further.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment: Mila is well integrated in the group and can participate positively in class. On the whole, she behaves appropriately and works well with the rest of the students, especially during practical and familiar activities. However, when she becomes frustrated or does not get her way, she may withdraw, refuse to participate, or show signs of anger, which can affect her focus during the lesson. She sometimes needs reminders to stay engaged and remain seated during story time. When she is calm, she responds well and shows a much more cooperative attitude.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.00    Good. Mila can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

Mila has made good progress this term and has shown that she can understand and use a great deal of the language worked on in class. Her ability to repeat structures, recognise vocabulary, and take part in action activities is very promising. With continued encouragement to manage moments of frustration and to stay engaged throughout the whole lesson, she will be able to keep building confidence and continue developing her English skills successfully.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The notes include moments of frustration and withdrawal — the report should handle these honestly while maintaining an encouraging tone. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Marks + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: encouraging and positive while honest about challenges - behaviour: frustration and withdrawal handled constructively, not ignored "}]

response.output:
```html

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Mila
  • Class: Oliver Thursday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Satisfactory

Comment:
Mila shows a generally positive attitude in class, particularly when activities align with her interests. She eagerly participates in hands-on tasks like the Dice Game, Family Bingo, and A Present for Me, where she demonstrates enthusiasm and a willingness to engage. Her motivation is strongest when she feels confident, such as when retelling stories or using familiar vocabulary and structures. However, Mila occasionally withdraws when faced with challenges or when things do not go her way—whether it’s not receiving the stamp, not being allowed to keep the blu-tack, or not having a clear view of the Big Book. These moments can lead to frustration, causing her to disengage temporarily, such as refusing to participate in the True or False activity or turning away during story time. Encouraging her resilience and reinforcing her many strengths—like her ability to combine moods and actions or her quick grasp of new vocabulary—will help her stay motivated even during difficult tasks.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:
Mila has made notable progress in her language acquisition, particularly in vocabulary and story retelling. She demonstrates a strong understanding of the Oliver story, often recounting it with minimal support, and excels in activities involving weather routines, clothing items, and color vocabulary (such as identifying white and black). Her ability to pair weather realia items with correct sentences and use structures like "putting on" and "taking off" (with occasional guidance) reflects her growing confidence. However, Mila sometimes struggles with patience during longer activities, as seen when the class grew restless by the end of the story. To build on her strengths, she should continue practising less internalised vocabulary, such as the words for "taking off" and "putting on," and work on maintaining focus during multi-step tasks. Her quick adaptation to new material, like describing what she’s wearing, is commendable and should be further nurtured.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Satisfactory

Comment:
Mila’s behaviour in class is generally cooperative, and she interacts well with her peers, especially during group activities like handing out materials. She shows moments of excellent participation, such as when she combined moods and actions effectively or completed worksheet tasks with ease. However, her mood can fluctuate—she may lay down, turn away, or express frustration (e.g., claiming a headache) when faced with minor setbacks or unmet expectations. These reactions sometimes disrupt her focus, as seen when she refused to engage in the True or False game or during story time. With gentle reminders and positive reinforcement, Mila quickly recovers her enthusiasm, as demonstrated when she rejoined activities after initial hesitation. Helping her develop strategies to manage frustration—such as asking for help or adjusting her position to see the Big Book—will support her in maintaining a consistent, positive presence in the classroom.

Final comment

________________________________________________________________________________
Mila has shown a mix of impressive strengths and areas for growth this term. Her ability to retell stories, use new vocabulary, and engage in interactive games highlights her potential and enthusiasm for learning. While she occasionally struggles with emotional regulation when faced with challenges, her willingness to re-engage—once supported—demonstrates her resilience. Continued practice with patience and focus, along with celebrating her many successes (like her mastery of color words and story structures), will help her build confidence and consistency. With her bright and participative spirit, Mila is well on her way to further progress in her language journey.

```

However, when she becomes frustrated or does not get her way, she may withdraw, refuse to participate, or show signs of anger, which can affect her focus during the lesson. She sometimes needs reminders to stay engaged and remain seated during story time. When she is calm, she responds well and shows a much more cooperative attitude.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.00    Good. Mila can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

Mila has made good progress this term and has shown that she can understand and use a great deal of the language worked on in class. Her ability to repeat structures, recognise vocabulary, and take part in action activities is very promising. With continued encouragement to manage moments of frustration and to stay engaged throughout the whole lesson, she will be able to keep building confidence and continue developing her English skills successfully.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The notes include moments of frustration and withdrawal — the report should handle these honestly while maintaining an encouraging tone. Is the group more easily distracted than normal? Is the student especially tired on rainy days, after public holidays, etc.? 14 LEARNING: definition: the process of studying, practising or experiencing whereby an individual or a group acquires specific knowledge, particularly knowledge required for learning an art form or profession. o Use of structures worked on during the story o Level of learning objectives achieved o Strengths o Weaknesses, together with a plan for improvement and commitment on behalf of the school to work on this aspect. Avoid repetitions in the same section. Keep an encouraging and positive tone Reference report (a known-good output for the same notes — use as a quality benchmark, student name may differ):

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Mila
  • Teacher: Thérèse Garnier
  • Level: Oliver Thursday 2025/2026
  • Period: 2026-02-22 to 2026-05-22

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment: Mila generally comes to class in a positive mood and shows interest in the activities presented. She is usually willing to participate and can be very engaged when she feels comfortable with the task, especially in action-based activities and games. At times, however, she can become upset when things do not go as she expects, which affects her willingness to continue taking part. With encouragement, she is able to rejoin the class and show good motivation, particularly when she has the opportunity to help or demonstrate what she knows.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment: Mila has shown good understanding of the structures and vocabulary worked on this term. She can repeat the target language well and, in many activities, uses the new language with confidence. Her knowledge of the story has improved notably, and she is able to retell parts of it with little support. She also performs very well in activities involving weather, clothing, and colour vocabulary. At times, she still needs help with some specific words and routines, such as taking off and putting on, and she may need further practice to remain consistent when new language is introduced. Continued repetition and reinforcement will help her consolidate her progress even further.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment: Mila is well integrated in the group and can participate positively in class. On the whole, she behaves appropriately and works well with the rest of the students, especially during practical and familiar activities. However, when she becomes frustrated or does not get her way, she may withdraw, refuse to participate, or show signs of anger, which can affect her focus during the lesson. She sometimes needs reminders to stay engaged and remain seated during story time. When she is calm, she responds well and shows a much more cooperative attitude.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.00    Good. Mila can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

Mila has made good progress this term and has shown that she can understand and use a great deal of the language worked on in class. Her ability to repeat structures, recognise vocabulary, and take part in action activities is very promising. With continued encouragement to manage moments of frustration and to stay engaged throughout the whole lesson, she will be able to keep building confidence and continue developing her English skills successfully.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The notes include moments of frustration and withdrawal — the report should handle these honestly while maintaining an encouraging tone. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Marks + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: encouraging and positive while honest about challenges - behaviour: frustration and withdrawal handled constructively, not ignored

response.output:
Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name:              Mila
Teacher:           Oliver Thursday
Level:              Oliver 2025/2026

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation):  Good

Comment:
Mila generally shows a happy and willing attitude in class, particularly when activities align with her interests, such as the present activity, Dice Game, and Family Bingo. She eagerly participates in action activities and demonstrates enthusiasm for songs, gestures, and storytelling. However, she sometimes closes up when she does not get her way, which temporarily affects her engagement. Mila’s willingness to help with materials and her ability to contribute to group tasks reflect her cooperative nature, though she occasionally holds back when frustrated.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process):  Very good

Comment:
Mila has shown strong progress in vocabulary and structure retention, particularly with weather routines, clothing items, and color words like white and black. She can repeat structures accurately and combine moods with actions effectively. Her ability to retell parts of the story with minimal help demonstrates her understanding of the material. While she occasionally needs support with specific words like "taking off" and "putting on," her overall comprehension and application of new vocabulary are commendable. Her performance in worksheet activities and pairing sentences with realia items highlights her growing confidence.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour):  Satisfactory

Comment:
Mila is generally well-integrated into the group and contributes positively to the classroom environment. However, she sometimes struggles with emotional regulation, such as when she could not see the Big Book or was not allowed to keep the blu-tack, leading her to withdraw temporarily from activities. She occasionally lays down or turns away when upset but responds well to redirection. Her behaviour improves when she feels included, and she demonstrates good focus during activities she enjoys. Encouraging her to manage frustration will help her maintain consistent participation.

---

Final comment

________________________________________________________________________________
Mila has made notable progress this term, particularly in vocabulary, structures, and storytelling. Her enthusiasm for engaging activities and her ability to apply learned material are strengths. While she sometimes needs support in managing emotions during challenges, her overall growth and willingness to participate are encouraging. Continued practice with new vocabulary and routines will help her build even greater confidence. Well done, Mila!

- behaviour: frustration and withdrawal handled constructively, not ignored

However, it omits the required Marks section entirely, which is a structural failure. Grounding is mostly acceptable, but there are some overstatements or weakly supported claims, such as consistent respect/cooperation with peers and active participation in the Dice Game despite notes saying she refused it on one occasion. It also uses an apparently incorrect level/year compared with the reference/notes context. Overall, it is positive and mostly traceable, but it does not fully meet the required structure and has minor grounding issues.

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

Mila demonstrates a positive attitude toward classroom activities, arriving in a happy mood and showing willingness to engage. She actively participates in tasks such as story retelling, action activities, and vocabulary exercises, applying previously acquired knowledge with enthusiasm. While she occasionally closes up when things do not go her way, her overall cooperation with peers and the teacher remains evident. Mila’s engagement in group activities, such as the Dice Game and Family Bingo, reflects her motivation to contribute and learn.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:

Mila has shown solid progress in acquiring vocabulary and using structures during storytelling and routines. She can combine moods with actions, repeat phrases accurately, and participate in activities like pairing sentences with weather items and clothing vocabulary. Her ability to retell parts of the story with minimal assistance highlights her understanding of the material. However, she occasionally struggles with specific phrases like “taking off” and “putting on” during weather routines and may need reminders to stay focused during longer activities.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Mila generally integrates well within the group and contributes positively to the classroom environment. She participates actively in most activities and shows respect toward peers and the teacher. While she has moments of frustration—such as when she couldn’t see the Big Book or when asked not to keep blu-tack—she manages these situations with guidance and returns to participation. Her behaviour during structured tasks like worksheets and games is commendable, though she may need occasional reminders to remain seated or engaged during storytelling.

Final comment

________________________________________________________________________________

Mila has shown steady progress this term, demonstrating enthusiasm and engagement in classroom activities. Her ability to use vocabulary and structures in context is developing well, and her willingness to participate—despite occasional frustrations—reflects a positive learning attitude. With continued practice and support, she will further strengthen her language skills and confidence. Keep up the great work, Mila!

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Mila
  • Teacher: Thérèse Garnier
  • Level: Oliver Thursday 2025/2026
  • Period: 2026-02-22 to 2026-05-22

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment: Mila generally comes to class in a positive mood and shows interest in the activities presented. She is usually willing to participate and can be very engaged when she feels comfortable with the task, especially in action-based activities and games. At times, however, she can become upset when things do not go as she expects, which affects her willingness to continue taking part. With encouragement, she is able to rejoin the class and show good motivation, particularly when she has the opportunity to help or demonstrate what she knows.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment: Mila has shown good understanding of the structures and vocabulary worked on this term. She can repeat the target language well and, in many activities, uses the new language with confidence. Her knowledge of the story has improved notably, and she is able to retell parts of it with little support. She also performs very well in activities involving weather, clothing, and colour vocabulary. At times, she still needs help with some specific words and routines, such as taking off and putting on, and she may need further practice to remain consistent when new language is introduced. Continued repetition and reinforcement will help her consolidate her progress even further.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment: Mila is well integrated in the group and can participate positively in class. On the whole, she behaves appropriately and works well with the rest of the students, especially during practical and familiar activities. However, when she becomes frustrated or does not get her way, she may withdraw, refuse to participate, or show signs of anger, which can affect her focus during the lesson. She sometimes needs reminders to stay engaged and remain seated during story time. When she is calm, she responds well and shows a much more cooperative attitude.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.00    Good. Mila can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

Mila has made good progress this term and has shown that she can understand and use a great deal of the language worked on in class. Her ability to repeat structures, recognise vocabulary, and take part in action activities is very promising. With continued encouragement to manage moments of frustration and to stay engaged throughout the whole lesson, she will be able to keep building confidence and continue developing her English skills successfully.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The notes include moments of frustration and withdrawal — the report should handle these honestly while maintaining an encouraging tone. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Marks + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: encouraging and positive while honest about challenges - behaviour: frustration and withdrawal handled constructively, not ignored "}]
