The notes include moments of frustration and withdrawal — the report should handle these honestly while maintaining an encouraging tone. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Marks + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: encouraging and positive while honest about challenges - behaviour: frustration and withdrawal handled constructively, not ignored

prompt.raw:
[{"content":"You are a report card writer for a school teacher. The student notes are the sole source of facts for the report. Every observation, data field, and mark must come from the notes — not from any examples. ## Style & Layout Guide The following are example report cards. Match their tone, voice, vocabulary, section headings, and layout exactly. These examples may differ slightly from the provided additional instructions - in that case, the instructions from the user take precedence. IMPORTANT: Do not copy specific Data field values, Marks, or observations from the examples — only include a Data field or Marks section if that value is present in the student notes. ### Example 1: pdfAvaluacioRo.pdf Kids&Us. Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term --- Term report (3rd term) Data Name: Romane Leclerc Teacher: Thérèse Garnier Level: Oliver 2024/2025 Absences: 1 Frequency of use (audio): Insufficient Participation/Motivation Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good Comment: Romane displays a keen enthusiasm for learning, particularly evident during vocabulary sessions. She consistently engages with activities, showcasing a willingness to assist in storytelling. Her high level of interest portrays a positive attitude, inspiring her classmates as they work together on different tasks and games. Romane's commitment to participating in exercises, even during challenging activities like singing and miming, reflects her motivation to succeed. Learning process Assessment (Learning process): Good Comment: Romane has made commendable progress in her vocabulary acquisition, particularly when discussing Oliver and his family members. She actively engages in activities such as story retelling and dice games, enhancing her understanding of the material. However, Romane has faced challenges with certain grammar concepts, including prepositions. It is essential for her to continue practising these areas to further expand her language skills and confidence in speaking. Behaviour Assessment (Behaviour): Very good Comment: Romane exhibits positive behaviour throughout class, interacting well with her peers and contributing to a collaborative environment. Her involvement in group activities encourages a supportive atmosphere. She shows respect to both classmates and the teacher and expresses encouragement towards others, supporting a healthy learning environment while still developing patience in group settings. Romane's respectful conduct plays an essential role in maintaining the class's focus. Marks Oral Test Mark: 8.00 Good. Romane can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct. --- Final comment ________________________________________________________________________________ Romane has had a successful year, showing significant growth in her English language skills. Her motivation and engagement in class activities are commendable, and she has gradually acquired vocabulary and grammar structures. Continued practice with audio materials over the summer will undoubtedly support her ongoing learning journey. I look forward to seeing her further develop her skills in the coming year. ### Example 2: pdfAvaluacioCl.pdf Kids&Us. Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term --- Term report (3rd term) Data Name: Clémence Legrand Teacher: Thérèse Garnier Level: Oliver 2024/2025 Absences: 3 Frequency of use (audio): Insufficient Participation/Motivation Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Satisfactory Comment: Clémence shows an acceptable level of motivation and enthusiasm during class activities, particularly during engaging games like the dice game. Although she encounters challenges with vocabulary, her willingness to participate is commendable. She attempts to interact with lesson materials and her peers despite some struggles. Encouragement to keep engaging positively will help her continue to grow and develop her confidence in classroom interactions. Learning process Assessment (Learning process): Poor Comment: Clémence is currently facing challenges with vocabulary, particularly words like young and old, as well as with constructing complete sentences. While she has shown understanding during specific activities, such as identifying clothing for different weather conditions, consistent attention to vocabulary is essential for her progress. To support her learning, it is recommended that Clémence continues to revisit vocabulary at home, helping her to build upon her knowledge and confidence in class. Behaviour Assessment (Behaviour): Good Comment: Clémence is generally a pleasant student but occasionally struggles to focus and stay on task. She sometimes needs reminders to pay attention and limit conversations with her friends during lessons. Despite these distractions, she listens well when prompted. Her behaviour towards group activities is friendly, and she interacts positively with her classmates. By fostering her ability to maintain focus, Clémence can further enhance her learning and classroom experience. Marks Oral Test Mark: 5.90 Needs to improve. Clémence shows a good understanding of the question, but needs to hear the sound of the first word from the teacher to provide an answer and uses only one word when answering. --- Final comment ____________________________________________________________ Clémence has encountered several difficulties this year, particularly with vocabulary retention and attention during class activities. While she displays enthusiasm in group settings, her distractibility hinders her progress. Moving forward, it's crucial that she continues to practise her vocabulary over the summer. With dedicated effort, she has the potential to improve her learning significantly. ### Example 3: pdfAvaluacioLyam.pdf Kids&Us. Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term --- Term report (3rd term) Data Name: Lyam Renard Meli Teacher: Thérèse Garnier Level: Oliver 2024/2025 Absences: 1 Frequency of use (audio): Insufficient Participation/Motivation Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Excellent Comment: Lyam continues to radiate enthusiasm in class, contributing positively to the learning environment. He eagerly participates in all activities, demonstrating a notable improvement in waiting his turn to speak, even when keen to share. His comfort within the group fosters motivation, making him enjoy lessons more. Lyam approaches every task with readiness and a positive mindset, ensuring his engagement levels remain high and inspiring his peers. Learning process Assessment (Learning process): Excellent Comment: Lyam's learning has been exemplary this year. He excels at retelling stories and has shown noticeable improvement in making complete sentences. His ability to describe characteristics of Oliver's family members, indicates his understanding of the material. He quickly identifies and corrects his mistakes, showcasing his awareness and dedication to learning. Overall, his level of engagement is excellent, and he enjoys demonstrating what he has learned during class. Behaviour Assessment (Behaviour): Very good Comment: Lyam exhibits excellent behaviour in the classroom, showing respect for his teachers and kindness towards classmates. He enjoys participating in activities, often assisting others when he can. His eagerness to take part is commendable, though he needs occasional reminders to wait for his turn to speak. Overall, his cooperative approach contributes positively to group dynamics and the classroom environment. Marks Oral Test Mark: 9.90 Excellent. Lyam can understand the question and, in most cases, answers using a structure which is complete and accurate. Excellent work! --- Final comment ____________________________________________ Lyam has had a wonderfully productive year, showcasing outstanding learning and behavioural qualities. He has made significant strides in both his participation and his ability to articulate thoughts in complete sentences. Well done, Lyam, and keep up this exceptional journey in learning. ## Important Additional Instructions from User - Takes precedence over any provided example MOTIVATION AND PARTICIPATION: o Motivation: definition: factor or group of factors that bring about a specific type of behaviour. This section should comment on whether the student shows a positive attitude towards different activities conducted in the classroom: ▪ How does the student come into the classroom? ▪ Do they show willingness to learn? ▪ Are they open to doing new activities? ▪ Are they cooperative with the rest of the group and with the teacher? o Participation: definition: involvement in an event, act or activity. Parents must be informed whether their child participates in the activities that are carried out in the classroom and whether or not they put previously-acquired knowledge, as well as language currently being worked on, into practice. ▪ Outline the degree of participation based on what the student views as being important. For example, he/she is very competitive and always wants to win. He/she gets cross when they don't win. He/she always wants to come first. ▪ Another noteworthy aspect is whether the student holds back when new material is introduced, and whether they take part to a lesser degree than when internalised knowledge is elicited in class. BEHAVIOUR: definition: the way in which a person behaves in a specific or general situation. o Note whether the student is integrated within the group or whether they are easily distracted or unable to focus. o Point out, if necessary, how a student is reacting in light of situations that have had to be tackled with a specific plan. o Explain whether the environment or general mood of the group, or the student's particular mood, affects the student's behaviour. Is the group more easily distracted than normal? Is the student especially tired on rainy days, after public holidays, etc.? 14 LEARNING: definition: the process of studying, practising or experiencing whereby an individual or a group acquires specific knowledge, particularly knowledge required for learning an art form or profession. o Use of structures worked on during the story o Level of learning objectives achieved o Strengths o Weaknesses, together with a plan for improvement and commitment on behalf of the school to work on this aspect. Avoid repetitions in the same section. Keep an encouraging and positive tone ## Student Notes (source of truth — all report content must derive from these) Student: Mila, Class: Oliver Thursday - 2026-05-21: Mila came to class in a very happy mood. She knew some vocabulary and could repeat the structures too. She needed help with the "taking off" and "putting on" words during the weather routines. She got upset because she could not see the Big Book during story time, but did not move to be able to see. She laid down a few times, was asked to sit, and turned her back to me during the whole story because she was angry. She could do the action activity quite well and was happy to participate. - 2026-05-14: The Oliver Thursday students were all great and participated in the activities very well. Mila did close up a bit when she did not get her way, but it got better. They all loved the present activity, all wanted to help hand out the material, and they all got a green face. - 2026-05-14: Mila only listened at first. She was a bit upset because she couldn’t do the stamp and then did not take part in the routines. She then said she had a headache and did take part in the activities. The class could tell who was missing and name the family member, repeat the correct structure each time, pair up the correct sentence with the weather realia item, and say most of the words, although they sometimes needed a bit of help. They needed to move a lot and seemed to get bored by the end of the story. - 2026-05-14: Mila could tell the story with almost no help. She got upset by the end of class because I did not allow her to keep the blu-tack. She refused to take part in the True or False activity and the Dice Game. - 2026-05-14: Mila came today. She knew most of the vocabulary, structures, songs, gestures, and story. They all loved A present for me, Dice Game, and Family Bingo. - 2026-05-14: Mila could combine the mood and the actions very well. She knew the phrase that went with her weather card, and she knew the story quite well. She could already tell part of it and repeat the rest. They learned how to say what they were wearing, and they learned the color white and the color black today. They could do the weather and clothing item combination very well, and they did the worksheet activity very, very well. They also showed that they knew the color vocabulary, which was great. All of them could do the color vocabulary, and I was really proud of them, and their behavior was relatively great today, so I was really happy with them. - 2026-05-14: Mila already knew the structures and understood how to use them, so she could repeat them very well. She could also use the new vocabulary words and understood how to use them during the activities. ## Task Write a report card narrative for this student based on the notes above. response.output:
Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Mila
Teacher: Thérèse Garnier
Level: Oliver 2024/2025

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

Mila demonstrates a positive attitude toward classroom activities, arriving in a happy mood and showing willingness to engage. She actively participates in tasks such as story retelling, action activities, and vocabulary exercises, applying previously acquired knowledge with enthusiasm. While she occasionally closes up when things do not go her way, her overall cooperation with peers and the teacher remains evident. Mila’s engagement in group activities, such as the Dice Game and Family Bingo, reflects her motivation to contribute and learn.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:

Mila has shown solid progress in acquiring vocabulary and using structures during storytelling and routines. She can combine moods with actions, repeat phrases accurately, and participate in activities like pairing sentences with weather items and clothing vocabulary. Her ability to retell parts of the story with minimal assistance highlights her understanding of the material. However, she occasionally struggles with specific phrases like “taking off” and “putting on” during weather routines and may need reminders to stay focused during longer activities.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Mila generally integrates well within the group and contributes positively to the classroom environment. She participates actively in most activities and shows respect toward peers and the teacher. While she has moments of frustration—such as when she couldn’t see the Big Book or when asked not to keep blu-tack—she manages these situations with guidance and returns to participation. Her behaviour during structured tasks like worksheets and games is commendable, though she may need occasional reminders to remain seated or engaged during storytelling.

Final comment

________________________________________________________________________________

Mila has shown steady progress this term, demonstrating enthusiasm and engagement in classroom activities. Her ability to use vocabulary and structures in context is developing well, and her willingness to participate—despite occasional frustrations—reflects a positive learning attitude. With continued practice and support, she will further strengthen her language skills and confidence. Keep up the great work, Mila!

[{"role":"system","content":"You are grading output according to a user-specified rubric. If the statement in the rubric is true, then the output passes the test. You respond with a JSON object with this structure: {reason: string, pass: boolean, score: number} Examples: Hello world Content contains a greeting {"reason": "the content contains the word 'Hello'", "pass": true, "score": 1.0} Avast ye swabs, repel the invaders! Does not speak like a pirate {"reason": "'avast ye' is a common pirate term", "pass": false, "score": 0.0}"},{"role":"user","content":"

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Mila

Level: Oliver Thursday

Participation/Motivation

Comment:

Mila comes to class in a very happy mood and shows willingness to take part in the activities. She enjoys helping hand out the material, is happy to participate in action activities, and joins in well with games such as the present activity, Family Bingo and the Dice Game. When she does not get her way, she can close up a little, but she generally settles once she is engaged in the lesson.

Learning process

Comment:

Mila knows most of the vocabulary, structures, songs, gestures and story, and she can tell the story with almost no help. She repeats the structures very well and already understands how to use the new vocabulary words during activities. She combines the mood and the actions very well, knows the phrase that goes with her weather card, and can pair the weather and clothing item combination very well. She also worked very well on the worksheet activity and showed that she knew the colour vocabulary, including white and black. She sometimes needs help with the words “taking off” and “putting on” during the weather routines.

Behaviour

Comment:

Mila is integrated within the group and can take part positively, but when she becomes upset her behaviour is affected. On some occasions she only listened at first and did not take part in the routines straight away. She was also upset when she could not see the Big Book during story time, and she laid down a few times, was asked to sit, and turned her back during the whole story. At times she refused to join activities such as True or False and the Dice Game, but once she settles she can participate well again.

Final comment

________________________________________________________________________________

Mila has shown strong understanding and use of the course language, and she can take part very well when she is settled. Her enthusiasm is clear in many activities, and her ability to repeat structures and tell the story is a strength. With continued support in managing frustration and in the weather routine words she still needs help with, she can keep building confidence and continue to make progress.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Mila
  • Teacher: Thérèse Garnier
  • Level: Oliver Thursday 2025/2026
  • Period: 2026-02-22 to 2026-05-22

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment: Mila generally comes to class in a positive mood and shows interest in the activities presented. She is usually willing to participate and can be very engaged when she feels comfortable with the task, especially in action-based activities and games. At times, however, she can become upset when things do not go as she expects, which affects her willingness to continue taking part. With encouragement, she is able to rejoin the class and show good motivation, particularly when she has the opportunity to help or demonstrate what she knows.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment: Mila has shown good understanding of the structures and vocabulary worked on this term. She can repeat the target language well and, in many activities, uses the new language with confidence. Her knowledge of the story has improved notably, and she is able to retell parts of it with little support. She also performs very well in activities involving weather, clothing, and colour vocabulary. At times, she still needs help with some specific words and routines, such as taking off and putting on, and she may need further practice to remain consistent when new language is introduced. Continued repetition and reinforcement will help her consolidate her progress even further.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment: Mila is well integrated in the group and can participate positively in class. On the whole, she behaves appropriately and works well with the rest of the students, especially during practical and familiar activities. However, when she becomes frustrated or does not get her way, she may withdraw, refuse to participate, or show signs of anger, which can affect her focus during the lesson. She sometimes needs reminders to stay engaged and remain seated during story time. When she is calm, she responds well and shows a much more cooperative attitude.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.00    Good. Mila can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

Mila has made good progress this term and has shown that she can understand and use a great deal of the language worked on in class. Her ability to repeat structures, recognise vocabulary, and take part in action activities is very promising. With continued encouragement to manage moments of frustration and to stay engaged throughout the whole lesson, she will be able to keep building confidence and continue developing her English skills successfully.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The notes include moments of frustration and withdrawal — the report should handle these honestly while maintaining an encouraging tone. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Marks + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: encouraging and positive while honest about challenges - behaviour: frustration and withdrawal handled constructively, not ignored "}]

response.output:
Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Mila

Level: Oliver Thursday

Participation/Motivation

Comment:

Mila comes to class in a very happy mood and shows willingness to take part in the activities. She enjoys helping hand out the material, is happy to participate in action activities, and joins in well with games such as the present activity, Family Bingo and the Dice Game. When she does not get her way, she can close up a little, but she generally settles once she is engaged in the lesson.

Learning process

Comment:

Mila knows most of the vocabulary, structures, songs, gestures and story, and she can tell the story with almost no help. She repeats the structures very well and already understands how to use the new vocabulary words during activities. She combines the mood and the actions very well, knows the phrase that goes with her weather card, and can pair the weather and clothing item combination very well. She also worked very well on the worksheet activity and showed that she knew the colour vocabulary, including white and black. She sometimes needs help with the words “taking off” and “putting on” during the weather routines.

Behaviour

Comment:

Mila is integrated within the group and can take part positively, but when she becomes upset her behaviour is affected. On some occasions she only listened at first and did not take part in the routines straight away. She was also upset when she could not see the Big Book during story time, and she laid down a few times, was asked to sit, and turned her back during the whole story. At times she refused to join activities such as True or False and the Dice Game, but once she settles she can participate well again.

Final comment

________________________________________________________________________________

Mila has shown strong understanding and use of the course language, and she can take part very well when she is settled. Her enthusiasm is clear in many activities, and her ability to repeat structures and tell the story is a strength. With continued support in managing frustration and in the weather routine words she still needs help with, she can keep building confidence and continue to make progress.

However, it does not include the required Marks section, so it fails the required structure. There are also some grounding issues: statements such as Mila 'eventually participating in all activities,' consistently interacting well with peers, and contributing to a collaborative environment are stronger than what the notes support, especially given that she refused some activities and did not take part in some routines. Term report (3rd term)

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

Mila demonstrates a positive attitude towards learning and actively engages in classroom activities. She comes to class with enthusiasm and shows a willingness to participate in new activities. Mila enjoys taking part in group tasks, such as helping to hand out materials and participating in games like "A present for me," Dice Game, and Family Bingo. While she occasionally closes up when she does not get her way, she generally recovers and continues to participate. Her eagerness to share her knowledge, such as telling the story with little assistance, reflects her motivation to succeed in her language learning journey.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:

Mila has made commendable progress in her language acquisition. She demonstrates a strong grasp of vocabulary and structures, often repeating them accurately during activities. Mila excels in storytelling, combining actions and moods effectively, and can retell parts of the story with minimal assistance. She actively participates in combining weather and clothing items and shows proficiency in color vocabulary. However, she occasionally requires support with specific phrases, such as "taking off" and "putting on," during weather routines. To further enhance her learning, continued practice with these phrases will support her overall language development and confidence in speaking.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Mila generally exhibits positive behaviour in the classroom. She interacts well with her peers and contributes to a collaborative environment, particularly during group activities. While she can become frustrated when things do not go her way—such as when she cannot see the Big Book during story time or keep the blu-tack—she manages to refocus and participate in activities. Her occasional need to lay down or turn her back during the story indicates moments of frustration, but these instances are followed by her re-engaging with the tasks. Mila’s willingness to help others and her eventual participation in all activities highlight her cooperative spirit and respect for the learning environment.

Final comment

________________________________________________________________________________

Mila has shown great enthusiasm and engagement in her learning this term. Her ability to grasp and use new vocabulary and structures is commendable, and she actively participates in classroom activities. While she occasionally faces moments of frustration, her willingness to overcome these challenges and re-engage with tasks demonstrates her resilience and positive attitude. Continued practice with specific phrases and activities will further support her language development. We look forward to seeing her progress and encourage her to keep up her excellent work.

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Mila
  • Teacher: Thérèse Garnier
  • Level: Oliver Thursday 2025/2026
  • Period: 2026-02-22 to 2026-05-22

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment: Mila generally comes to class in a positive mood and shows interest in the activities presented. She is usually willing to participate and can be very engaged when she feels comfortable with the task, especially in action-based activities and games. At times, however, she can become upset when things do not go as she expects, which affects her willingness to continue taking part. With encouragement, she is able to rejoin the class and show good motivation, particularly when she has the opportunity to help or demonstrate what she knows.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment: Mila has shown good understanding of the structures and vocabulary worked on this term. She can repeat the target language well and, in many activities, uses the new language with confidence. Her knowledge of the story has improved notably, and she is able to retell parts of it with little support. She also performs very well in activities involving weather, clothing, and colour vocabulary. At times, she still needs help with some specific words and routines, such as taking off and putting on, and she may need further practice to remain consistent when new language is introduced. Continued repetition and reinforcement will help her consolidate her progress even further.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment: Mila is well integrated in the group and can participate positively in class. On the whole, she behaves appropriately and works well with the rest of the students, especially during practical and familiar activities. However, when she becomes frustrated or does not get her way, she may withdraw, refuse to participate, or show signs of anger, which can affect her focus during the lesson. She sometimes needs reminders to stay engaged and remain seated during story time. When she is calm, she responds well and shows a much more cooperative attitude.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.00    Good. Mila can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

Mila has made good progress this term and has shown that she can understand and use a great deal of the language worked on in class. Her ability to repeat structures, recognise vocabulary, and take part in action activities is very promising. With continued encouragement to manage moments of frustration and to stay engaged throughout the whole lesson, she will be able to keep building confidence and continue developing her English skills successfully.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The notes include moments of frustration and withdrawal — the report should handle these honestly while maintaining an encouraging tone. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Marks + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: encouraging and positive while honest about challenges - behaviour: frustration and withdrawal handled constructively, not ignored "}]

She also performs very well in activities involving weather, clothing, and colour vocabulary. At times, she still needs help with some specific words and routines, such as taking off and putting on, and she may need further practice to remain consistent when new language is introduced. Continued repetition and reinforcement will help her consolidate her progress even further.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment: Mila is well integrated in the group and can participate positively in class. On the whole, she behaves appropriately and works well with the rest of the students, especially during practical and familiar activities. However, when she becomes frustrated or does not get her way, she may withdraw, refuse to participate, or show signs of anger, which can affect her focus during the lesson. She sometimes needs reminders to stay engaged and remain seated during story time. When she is calm, she responds well and shows a much more cooperative attitude.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.00    Good. Mila can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

Mila has made good progress this term and has shown that she can understand and use a great deal of the language worked on in class. Her ability to repeat structures, recognise vocabulary, and take part in action activities is very promising. With continued encouragement to manage moments of frustration and to stay engaged throughout the whole lesson, she will be able to keep building confidence and continue developing her English skills successfully.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The notes include moments of frustration and withdrawal — the report should handle these honestly while maintaining an encouraging tone. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Marks + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: encouraging and positive while honest about challenges - behaviour: frustration and withdrawal handled constructively, not ignored

[{"content":"You are a report card writer for a school teacher. The student notes are the sole source of facts for the report. Every observation, data field, and mark must come from the notes — not from any examples. ## Style & Layout Guide The following are example report cards. Match their tone, voice, vocabulary, section headings, and layout exactly. These examples may differ slightly from the provided additional instructions - in that case, the instructions from the user take precedence. IMPORTANT: Do not copy specific Data field values, Marks, or observations from the examples — only include a Data field or Marks section if that value is present in the student notes. ### Example 1: pdfAvaluacioRo.pdf Kids&Us. Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term --- Term report (3rd term) Data Name: Romane Leclerc Teacher: Thérèse Garnier Level: Oliver 2024/2025 Absences: 1 Frequency of use (audio): Insufficient Participation/Motivation Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good Comment: Romane displays a keen enthusiasm for learning, particularly evident during vocabulary sessions. She consistently engages with activities, showcasing a willingness to assist in storytelling. Her high level of interest portrays a positive attitude, inspiring her classmates as they work together on different tasks and games. Romane's commitment to participating in exercises, even during challenging activities like singing and miming, reflects her motivation to succeed. Learning process Assessment (Learning process): Good Comment: Romane has made commendable progress in her vocabulary acquisition, particularly when discussing Oliver and his family members. She actively engages in activities such as story retelling and dice games, enhancing her understanding of the material. However, Romane has faced challenges with certain grammar concepts, including prepositions. It is essential for her to continue practising these areas to further expand her language skills and confidence in speaking. Behaviour Assessment (Behaviour): Very good Comment: Romane exhibits positive behaviour throughout class, interacting well with her peers and contributing to a collaborative environment. Her involvement in group activities encourages a supportive atmosphere. She shows respect to both classmates and the teacher and expresses encouragement towards others, supporting a healthy learning environment while still developing patience in group settings. Romane's respectful conduct plays an essential role in maintaining the class's focus. Marks Oral Test Mark: 8.00 Good. Romane can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct. --- Final comment ________________________________________________________________________________ Romane has had a successful year, showing significant growth in her English language skills. Her motivation and engagement in class activities are commendable, and she has gradually acquired vocabulary and grammar structures. Continued practice with audio materials over the summer will undoubtedly support her ongoing learning journey. I look forward to seeing her further develop her skills in the coming year. ### Example 2: pdfAvaluacioCl.pdf Kids&Us. Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term --- Term report (3rd term) Data Name: Clémence Legrand Teacher: Thérèse Garnier Level: Oliver 2024/2025 Absences: 3 Frequency of use (audio): Insufficient Participation/Motivation Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Satisfactory Comment: Clémence shows an acceptable level of motivation and enthusiasm during class activities, particularly during engaging games like the dice game. Although she encounters challenges with vocabulary, her willingness to participate is commendable. She attempts to interact with lesson materials and her peers despite some struggles. Encouragement to keep engaging positively will help her continue to grow and develop her confidence in classroom interactions. Learning process Assessment (Learning process): Poor Comment: Clémence is currently facing challenges with vocabulary, particularly words like young and old, as well as with constructing complete sentences. While she has shown understanding during specific activities, such as identifying clothing for different weather conditions, consistent attention to vocabulary is essential for her progress. To support her learning, it is recommended that Clémence continues to revisit vocabulary at home, helping her to build upon her knowledge and confidence in class. Behaviour Assessment (Behaviour): Good Comment: Clémence is generally a pleasant student but occasionally struggles to focus and stay on task. She sometimes needs reminders to pay attention and limit conversations with her friends during lessons. Despite these distractions, she listens well when prompted. Her behaviour towards group activities is friendly, and she interacts positively with her classmates. By fostering her ability to maintain focus, Clémence can further enhance her learning and classroom experience. Marks Oral Test Mark: 5.90 Needs to improve. Clémence shows a good understanding of the question, but needs to hear the sound of the first word from the teacher to provide an answer and uses only one word when answering. --- Final comment ____________________________________________________________ Clémence has encountered several difficulties this year, particularly with vocabulary retention and attention during class activities. While she displays enthusiasm in group settings, her distractibility hinders her progress. Moving forward, it's crucial that she continues to practise her vocabulary over the summer. With dedicated effort, she has the potential to improve her learning significantly. ### Example 3: pdfAvaluacioLyam.pdf Kids&Us. Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term --- Term report (3rd term) Data Name: Lyam Renard Meli Teacher: Thérèse Garnier Level: Oliver 2024/2025 Absences: 1 Frequency of use (audio): Insufficient Participation/Motivation Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Excellent Comment: Lyam continues to radiate enthusiasm in class, contributing positively to the learning environment. He eagerly participates in all activities, demonstrating a notable improvement in waiting his turn to speak, even when keen to share. His comfort within the group fosters motivation, making him enjoy lessons more. Lyam approaches every task with readiness and a positive mindset, ensuring his engagement levels remain high and inspiring his peers. Learning process Assessment (Learning process): Excellent Comment: Lyam's learning has been exemplary this year. He excels at retelling stories and has shown noticeable improvement in making complete sentences. His ability to describe characteristics of Oliver's family members, indicates his understanding of the material. He quickly identifies and corrects his mistakes, showcasing his awareness and dedication to learning. Overall, his level of engagement is excellent, and he enjoys demonstrating what he has learned during class. Behaviour Assessment (Behaviour): Very good Comment: Lyam exhibits excellent behaviour in the classroom, showing respect for his teachers and kindness towards classmates. He enjoys participating in activities, often assisting others when he can. His eagerness to take part is commendable, though he needs occasional reminders to wait for his turn to speak. Overall, his cooperative approach contributes positively to group dynamics and the classroom environment. Marks Oral Test Mark: 9.90 Excellent. Lyam can understand the question and, in most cases, answers using a structure which is complete and accurate. Excellent work! --- Final comment ____________________________________________ Lyam has had a wonderfully productive year, showcasing outstanding learning and behavioural qualities. He has made significant strides in both his participation and his ability to articulate thoughts in complete sentences. Well done, Lyam, and keep up this exceptional journey in learning. ## Important Additional Instructions from User - Takes precedence over any provided example MOTIVATION AND PARTICIPATION: o Motivation: definition: factor or group of factors that bring about a specific type of behaviour. This section should comment on whether the student shows a positive attitude towards different activities conducted in the classroom: ▪ How does the student come into the classroom? ▪ Do they show willingness to learn? ▪ Are they open to doing new activities? ▪ Are they cooperative with the rest of the group and with the teacher? o Participation: definition: involvement in an event, act or activity. Parents must be informed whether their child participates in the activities that are carried out in the classroom and whether or not they put previously-acquired knowledge, as well as language currently being worked on, into practice. ▪ Outline the degree of participation based on what the student views as being important. For example, he/she is very competitive and always wants to win. He/she gets cross when they don't win. He/she always wants to come first. ▪ Another noteworthy aspect is whether the student holds back when new material is introduced, and whether they take part to a lesser degree than when internalised knowledge is elicited in class. BEHAVIOUR: definition: the way in which a person behaves in a specific or general situation. o Note whether the student is integrated within the group or whether they are easily distracted or unable to focus. o Point out, if necessary, how a student is reacting in light of situations that have had to be tackled with a specific plan. o Explain whether the environment or general mood of the group, or the student's particular mood, affects the student's behaviour. Is the group more easily distracted than normal? Is the student especially tired on rainy days, after public holidays, etc.? 14 LEARNING: definition: the process of studying, practising or experiencing whereby an individual or a group acquires specific knowledge, particularly knowledge required for learning an art form or profession. o Use of structures worked on during the story o Level of learning objectives achieved o Strengths o Weaknesses, together with a plan for improvement and commitment on behalf of the school to work on this aspect. Avoid repetitions in the same section. Keep an encouraging and positive tone ## Student Notes (source of truth — all report content must derive from these) Student: Mila, Class: Oliver Thursday - 2026-05-21: Mila came to class in a very happy mood. She knew some vocabulary and could repeat the structures too. She needed help with the "taking off" and "putting on" words during the weather routines. She got upset because she could not see the Big Book during story time, but did not move to be able to see. She laid down a few times, was asked to sit, and turned her back to me during the whole story because she was angry. She could do the action activity quite well and was happy to participate. - 2026-05-14: The Oliver Thursday students were all great and participated in the activities very well. Mila did close up a bit when she did not get her way, but it got better. They all loved the present activity, all wanted to help hand out the material, and they all got a green face. - 2026-05-14: Mila only listened at first. She was a bit upset because she couldn’t do the stamp and then did not take part in the routines. She then said she had a headache and did take part in the activities. The class could tell who was missing and name the family member, repeat the correct structure each time, pair up the correct sentence with the weather realia item, and say most of the words, although they sometimes needed a bit of help. They needed to move a lot and seemed to get bored by the end of the story. - 2026-05-14: Mila could tell the story with almost no help. She got upset by the end of class because I did not allow her to keep the blu-tack. She refused to take part in the True or False activity and the Dice Game. - 2026-05-14: Mila came today. She knew most of the vocabulary, structures, songs, gestures, and story. They all loved A present for me, Dice Game, and Family Bingo. - 2026-05-14: Mila could combine the mood and the actions very well. She knew the phrase that went with her weather card, and she knew the story quite well. She could already tell part of it and repeat the rest. They learned how to say what they were wearing, and they learned the color white and the color black today. They could do the weather and clothing item combination very well, and they did the worksheet activity very, very well. They also showed that they knew the color vocabulary, which was great. All of them could do the color vocabulary, and I was really proud of them, and their behavior was relatively great today, so I was really happy with them. - 2026-05-14: Mila already knew the structures and understood how to use them, so she could repeat them very well. She could also use the new vocabulary words and understood how to use them during the activities. ## Task Write a report card narrative for this student based on the notes above. Output the report as clean HTML (using

,

,

  • tags as appropriate). Do not include , , or wrapper tags — just the content HTML. Only include structured Data fields (Absences, Marks, Frequency of use, etc.) if those values are explicitly present in the notes. Do not invent or carry over values from the examples. Do not replicate their specific Data field values, Marks, or observations — the report content comes only from the student notes above. ","role":"user"}]

response.output:
```html

Term report (3rd term)

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

Mila demonstrates a positive attitude towards learning and actively engages in classroom activities. She comes to class with enthusiasm and shows a willingness to participate in new activities. Mila enjoys taking part in group tasks, such as helping to hand out materials and participating in games like "A present for me," Dice Game, and Family Bingo. While she occasionally closes up when she does not get her way, she generally recovers and continues to participate. Her eagerness to share her knowledge, such as telling the story with little assistance, reflects her motivation to succeed in her language learning journey.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:

Mila has made commendable progress in her language acquisition. She demonstrates a strong grasp of vocabulary and structures, often repeating them accurately during activities. Mila excels in storytelling, combining actions and moods effectively, and can retell parts of the story with minimal assistance. She actively participates in combining weather and clothing items and shows proficiency in color vocabulary. However, she occasionally requires support with specific phrases, such as "taking off" and "putting on," during weather routines. To further enhance her learning, continued practice with these phrases will support her overall language development and confidence in speaking.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Mila generally exhibits positive behaviour in the classroom. She interacts well with her peers and contributes to a collaborative environment, particularly during group activities. While she can become frustrated when things do not go her way—such as when she cannot see the Big Book during story time or keep the blu-tack—she manages to refocus and participate in activities. Her occasional need to lay down or turn her back during the story indicates moments of frustration, but these instances are followed by her re-engaging with the tasks. Mila’s willingness to help others and her eventual participation in all activities highlight her cooperative spirit and respect for the learning environment.

Final comment

________________________________________________________________________________

Mila has shown great enthusiasm and engagement in her learning this term. Her ability to grasp and use new vocabulary and structures is commendable, and she actively participates in classroom activities. While she occasionally faces moments of frustration, her willingness to overcome these challenges and re-engage with tasks demonstrates her resilience and positive attitude. Continued practice with specific phrases and activities will further support her language development. We look forward to seeing her progress and encourage her to keep up her excellent work.

```

Parents must be informed whether their child participates in the activities that are carried out in the classroom and whether or not they put previously-acquired knowledge, as well as language currently being worked on, into practice. ▪ Outline the degree of participation based on what the student views as being important. For example, he/she is very competitive and always wants to win. He/she gets cross when they don't win. He/she always wants to come first. ▪ Another noteworthy aspect is whether the student holds back when new material is introduced, and whether they take part to a lesser degree than when internalised knowledge is elicited in class. BEHAVIOUR: definition: the way in which a person behaves in a specific or general situation. o Note whether the student is integrated within the group or whether they are easily distracted or unable to focus. o Point out, if necessary, how a student is reacting in light of situations that have had to be tackled with a specific plan. o Explain whether the environment or general mood of the group, or the student's particular mood, affects the student's behaviour. Is the group more easily distracted than normal? Is the student especially tired on rainy days, after public holidays, etc.? 14 LEARNING: definition: the process of studying, practising or experiencing whereby an individual or a group acquires specific knowledge, particularly knowledge required for learning an art form or profession. o Use of structures worked on during the story o Level of learning objectives achieved o Strengths o Weaknesses, together with a plan for improvement and commitment on behalf of the school to work on this aspect. Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Camille
  • Teacher: Thérèse Garnier
  • Level: Oliver-Group 2 Saturday 2025/2026
  • Absences: 0
  • Frequency of use (audio): Pass

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Camille comes into class with a calm and positive attitude, and she shows a pleasant willingness to take part in the different activities proposed. She is generally attentive and open to new tasks, enjoying the story work, the worksheet activities and games such as Present for me, Who’s Missing and the Dice Game. Camille participates well when she feels comfortable, and she shows interest in the language being worked on, especially when she can recall vocabulary and repeat what she has learned. At times she can be a little more hesitant, but with encouragement she responds well and continues to engage positively with the group.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:
Camille has shown a good understanding of the story and the language structures worked on this term. She can remember vocabulary words very well, especially colours, clothing items and some prepositions, and she also demonstrated understanding in activities about what Oliver and Baby could or could not do. She was able to recite parts of the story and to repeat when asked, showing that she is memorising the language and building her confidence. She sometimes needs help to recall the correct structures, particularly when asking questions or using the new language independently, but she shows real understanding and is making steady progress. Continued practice will help her become more secure and more confident when using the structures spontaneously.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Very good

Comment:
Camille has a peaceful, easy-going and attentive manner in class. She is generally well integrated within the group and her calm presence helps create a positive atmosphere. She is usually focused and listens carefully, showing a good level of concentration during activities. On occasion she can be a little playful with the materials or slightly distracted with a classmate, but this does not affect her overall behaviour, which remains very good. Camille responds well to guidance, follows the classroom routine and shows a kind and cooperative attitude towards both the teacher and her peers.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.10  Good. Camille can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

____________________________________________________________
Camille has had a positive term and has shown good understanding of the story content and vocabulary worked on in class. Her calm and attentive nature, together with her willingness to participate, have contributed well to the group. She is progressing nicely and, with continued practice and confidence, she will keep developing her language skills next term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The student profile is calm, attentive, and generally positive — the report should maintain a warm and encouraging tone. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Marks + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: warm, encouraging, matches Oliver-level examples - accuracy: occasional playful behaviour noted without exaggeration "}]

response.output:
```html

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Camille
Teacher: Thérèse Garnier
Level: Oliver-Group 2 Saturday 2024/2025

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:
Camille shows a positive attitude and enthusiasm in class, particularly during activities like "A present for me," Family Bingo, and the Dice Game. She enjoys listening to the story and demonstrates willingness to participate, such as repeating when asked. Her engagement is evident in her ability to recall vocabulary and structures, though she occasionally holds back when less confident. With encouragement, she regains her motivation and contributes positively to the group.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:
Camille has shown a good understanding of the material, including vocabulary for colors, clothing items, and some prepositions. She can recite parts of the story and demonstrates memorisation of key terms. While she and the group needed reminders for correct structures, she displayed a solid grasp of the worksheet activities, such as identifying what Oliver and Baby could or couldn’t do. She is still learning to ask questions about physical appearance before guessing, which remains a work in progress.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Camille is generally attentive and integrates well with the group, though she occasionally engages in playful distractions with peers, such as hiding materials jokingly. She responds well to encouragement and maintains a happy and cooperative demeanor. Her behaviour contributes positively to the classroom environment, and she shows pride in her work, as seen in her enjoyment of new routines and stories.

Final comment

____________________________________________________________

Camille has had a productive term, demonstrating enthusiasm and a good understanding of the material. She engages well in activities and shows a strong grasp of vocabulary and story elements. While she occasionally needs reminders for structures and can be hesitant, her overall attitude and participation are commendable. Continued practice and encouragement will help her build confidence and refine her language skills further.

```

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Camille
  • Teacher: [Teacher's name not provided in notes]
  • Level: Oliver-Group 2 Saturday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

Camille demonstrates a positive and engaged attitude in class, particularly during activities she enjoys. She shows enthusiasm for interactive tasks such as A Present for Me, the Dice Game, and Family Bingo, where her participation is active and willing. While she occasionally engages in playful distractions with peers—such as jokingly hiding materials—she generally responds well to encouragement and remains attentive during storytelling and routines. Her willingness to repeat phrases and engage with new material, like the weather and mood routines, reflects her openness to learning. However, her confidence fluctuates; at times, she appears hesitant, though she quickly regains her footing with support. Camille’s cooperative spirit and enjoyment of group activities contribute positively to the classroom dynamic.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:

Camille has made steady progress in her understanding of the course material. She retains vocabulary well, particularly colors, clothing items, and some prepositions, and she can partially recite stories from memory. Her comprehension is evident in worksheet activities, such as identifying what characters can or cannot do, though she still relies on reminders for correct sentence structures. While she grasps new routines—like miming weather and actions—quickly, she is still developing her ability to form questions about physical appearance before jumping to conclusions. Her memorization of vocabulary and engagement with storytelling indicate strengths in auditory and visual learning, but continued practice in structuring questions and sentences will help solidify her skills. Encouragement to apply these structures independently will further her confidence and accuracy.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Camille is generally well-behaved and integrates smoothly into the group. She interacts playfully with classmates, such as Zoé, though this occasionally leads to minor distractions—like delaying the return of materials—which are handled lightheartedly. Her attentiveness is consistent, especially during activities she enjoys, and she follows classroom routines with enthusiasm. While she may show moments of hesitation or reduced confidence, her overall demeanor remains positive and cooperative. Camille’s behaviour reflects a balance of engagement and youthful energy, and her ability to refocus when guided ensures she contributes constructively to the learning environment.

Final comment

____________________________________________________________

Camille has had a productive term, displaying a keen interest in interactive activities and a growing command of vocabulary and routines. Her enthusiasm for games like A Present for Me and the Dice Game highlights her strengths in participatory learning, while her occasional playful distractions remind us of her sociable nature. Moving forward, reinforcing sentence structures and questioning techniques will help her build on her solid foundation. With her positive attitude and willingness to engage, Camille is well-positioned to continue her progress. Encouraging her to practise these skills over the summer—especially through storytelling and audio resources—will further boost her confidence and fluency in the coming year.

BEHAVIOUR: definition: the way in which a person behaves in a specific or general situation. o Note whether the student is integrated within the group or whether they are easily distracted or unable to focus. o Point out, if necessary, how a student is reacting in light of situations that have had to be tackled with a specific plan. o Explain whether the environment or general mood of the group, or the student's particular mood, affects the student's behaviour. Is the group more easily distracted than normal? Data

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Camille comes into class with a calm and positive attitude, and she shows a pleasant willingness to take part in the different activities proposed. She is generally attentive and open to new tasks, enjoying the story work, the worksheet activities and games such as Present for me, Who’s Missing and the Dice Game. Camille participates well when she feels comfortable, and she shows interest in the language being worked on, especially when she can recall vocabulary and repeat what she has learned. At times she can be a little more hesitant, but with encouragement she responds well and continues to engage positively with the group.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:
Camille has shown a good understanding of the story and the language structures worked on this term. She can remember vocabulary words very well, especially colours, clothing items and some prepositions, and she also demonstrated understanding in activities about what Oliver and Baby could or could not do. She was able to recite parts of the story and to repeat when asked, showing that she is memorising the language and building her confidence. She sometimes needs help to recall the correct structures, particularly when asking questions or using the new language independently, but she shows real understanding and is making steady progress. Continued practice will help her become more secure and more confident when using the structures spontaneously.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Very good

Comment:
Camille has a peaceful, easy-going and attentive manner in class. She is generally well integrated within the group and her calm presence helps create a positive atmosphere. She is usually focused and listens carefully, showing a good level of concentration during activities. On occasion she can be a little playful with the materials or slightly distracted with a classmate, but this does not affect her overall behaviour, which remains very good. Camille responds well to guidance, follows the classroom routine and shows a kind and cooperative attitude towards both the teacher and her peers.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.10  Good. Camille can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

____________________________________________________________
Camille has had a positive term and has shown good understanding of the story content and vocabulary worked on in class. Her calm and attentive nature, together with her willingness to participate, have contributed well to the group. She is progressing nicely and, with continued practice and confidence, she will keep developing her language skills next term.

Camille has shown a good understanding of the story and the language structures worked on this term. She can remember vocabulary words very well, especially colours, clothing items and some prepositions, and she also demonstrated understanding in activities about what Oliver and Baby could or could not do. She was able to recite parts of the story and to repeat when asked, showing that she is memorising the language and building her confidence. She sometimes needs help to recall the correct structures, particularly when asking questions or using the new language independently, but she shows real understanding and is making steady progress. Continued practice will help her become more secure and more confident when using the structures spontaneously.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Very good

Comment:
Camille has a peaceful, easy-going and attentive manner in class. She is generally well integrated within the group and her calm presence helps create a positive atmosphere. She is usually focused and listens carefully, showing a good level of concentration during activities. On occasion she can be a little playful with the materials or slightly distracted with a classmate, but this does not affect her overall behaviour, which remains very good. Camille responds well to guidance, follows the classroom routine and shows a kind and cooperative attitude towards both the teacher and her peers.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.10  Good. Camille can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

____________________________________________________________
Camille has had a positive term and has shown good understanding of the story content and vocabulary worked on in class. Her calm and attentive nature, together with her willingness to participate, have contributed well to the group. She is progressing nicely and, with continued practice and confidence, she will keep developing her language skills next term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The student profile is calm, attentive, and generally positive — the report should maintain a warm and encouraging tone. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Marks + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: warm, encouraging, matches Oliver-level examples - accuracy: occasional playful behaviour noted without exaggeration

response.output:
```html

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Camille
  • Teacher: [Teacher's name not provided in notes]
  • Level: Oliver-Group 2 Saturday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

Camille demonstrates a positive and engaged attitude in class, particularly during activities she enjoys. She shows enthusiasm for interactive tasks such as A Present for Me, the Dice Game, and Family Bingo, where her participation is active and willing. While she occasionally engages in playful distractions with peers—such as jokingly hiding materials—she generally responds well to encouragement and remains attentive during storytelling and routines. Her willingness to repeat phrases and engage with new material, like the weather and mood routines, reflects her openness to learning. However, her confidence fluctuates; at times, she appears hesitant, though she quickly regains her footing with support. Camille’s cooperative spirit and enjoyment of group activities contribute positively to the classroom dynamic.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:

Camille has made steady progress in her understanding of the course material. She retains vocabulary well, particularly colors, clothing items, and some prepositions, and she can partially recite stories from memory. Her comprehension is evident in worksheet activities, such as identifying what characters can or cannot do, though she still relies on reminders for correct sentence structures. While she grasps new routines—like miming weather and actions—quickly, she is still developing her ability to form questions about physical appearance before jumping to conclusions. Her memorization of vocabulary and engagement with storytelling indicate strengths in auditory and visual learning, but continued practice in structuring questions and sentences will help solidify her skills. Encouragement to apply these structures independently will further her confidence and accuracy.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Camille is generally well-behaved and integrates smoothly into the group. She interacts playfully with classmates, such as Zoé, though this occasionally leads to minor distractions—like delaying the return of materials—which are handled lightheartedly. Her attentiveness is consistent, especially during activities she enjoys, and she follows classroom routines with enthusiasm. While she may show moments of hesitation or reduced confidence, her overall demeanor remains positive and cooperative. Camille’s behaviour reflects a balance of engagement and youthful energy, and her ability to refocus when guided ensures she contributes constructively to the learning environment.

Final comment

____________________________________________________________

Camille has had a productive term, displaying a keen interest in interactive activities and a growing command of vocabulary and routines. Her enthusiasm for games like A Present for Me and the Dice Game highlights her strengths in participatory learning, while her occasional playful distractions remind us of her sociable nature. Moving forward, reinforcing sentence structures and questioning techniques will help her build on her solid foundation. With her positive attitude and willingness to engage, Camille is well-positioned to continue her progress. Encouraging her to practise these skills over the summer—especially through storytelling and audio resources—will further boost her confidence and fluency in the coming year.

```

Parents must be informed whether their child participates in the activities that are carried out in the classroom and whether or not they put previously-acquired knowledge, as well as language currently being worked on, into practice. ▪ Outline the degree of participation based on what the student views as being important. For example, he/she is very competitive and always wants to win. He/she gets cross when they don't win. He/she always wants to come first. ▪ Another noteworthy aspect is whether the student holds back when new material is introduced, and whether they take part to a lesser degree than when internalised knowledge is elicited in class. BEHAVIOUR: definition: the way in which a person behaves in a specific or general situation. o Note whether the student is integrated within the group or whether they are easily distracted or unable to focus. o Point out, if necessary, how a student is reacting in light of situations that have had to be tackled with a specific plan. o Explain whether the environment or general mood of the group, or the student's particular mood, affects the student's behaviour. Is the group more easily distracted than normal? Is the student especially tired on rainy days, after public holidays, etc.? 14 LEARNING: definition: the process of studying, practising or experiencing whereby an individual or a group acquires specific knowledge, particularly knowledge required for learning an art form or profession. o Use of structures worked on during the story o Level of learning objectives achieved o Strengths o Weaknesses, together with a plan for improvement and commitment on behalf of the school to work on this aspect. Try not to avoid repetition in the same section Reference report (a known-good output for the same notes — use as a quality benchmark, student name may differ):

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Camille
  • Teacher: Thérèse Garnier
  • Level: Oliver-Group 2 Saturday 2025/2026
  • Absences: 0
  • Frequency of use (audio): Pass

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Camille comes into class with a calm and positive attitude, and she shows a pleasant willingness to take part in the different activities proposed. She is generally attentive and open to new tasks, enjoying the story work, the worksheet activities and games such as Present for me, Who’s Missing and the Dice Game. Camille participates well when she feels comfortable, and she shows interest in the language being worked on, especially when she can recall vocabulary and repeat what she has learned. At times she can be a little more hesitant, but with encouragement she responds well and continues to engage positively with the group.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:
Camille has shown a good understanding of the story and the language structures worked on this term. She can remember vocabulary words very well, especially colours, clothing items and some prepositions, and she also demonstrated understanding in activities about what Oliver and Baby could or could not do. She was able to recite parts of the story and to repeat when asked, showing that she is memorising the language and building her confidence. She sometimes needs help to recall the correct structures, particularly when asking questions or using the new language independently, but she shows real understanding and is making steady progress. Continued practice will help her become more secure and more confident when using the structures spontaneously.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Very good

Comment:
Camille has a peaceful, easy-going and attentive manner in class. She is generally well integrated within the group and her calm presence helps create a positive atmosphere. She is usually focused and listens carefully, showing a good level of concentration during activities. On occasion she can be a little playful with the materials or slightly distracted with a classmate, but this does not affect her overall behaviour, which remains very good. Camille responds well to guidance, follows the classroom routine and shows a kind and cooperative attitude towards both the teacher and her peers.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.10  Good. Camille can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

____________________________________________________________
Camille has had a positive term and has shown good understanding of the story content and vocabulary worked on in class. Her calm and attentive nature, together with her willingness to participate, have contributed well to the group. She is progressing nicely and, with continued practice and confidence, she will keep developing her language skills next term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The student profile is calm, attentive, and generally positive — the report should maintain a warm and encouraging tone. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Marks + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: warm, encouraging, matches Oliver-level examples - accuracy: occasional playful behaviour noted without exaggeration "}]

response.output:
Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Camille
Teacher: [Not provided in notes]
Level: Oliver-Group 2 Saturday
Absences: [Not provided in notes]
Frequency of use (audio): [Not provided in notes]

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

  • Camille demonstrated attentiveness in most activities, particularly enjoying the "Present for me" and "Who’s missing" games.
  • She showed willingness to participate, reciting parts of the story and engaging with the worksheet activities about what Oliver and Baby could or couldn’t do.
  • She was involved in playful interactions with Zoé but remained generally attentive overall.
  • Camille quickly understood new routines, such as the weather and action miming, as well as the mood routine with 'he's smiling because he's happy.'
  • While she was hesitant at times, encouragement helped her regain confidence and feel happy with her progress.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:

  • Camille demonstrated a solid understanding of vocabulary, particularly colors, clothing items, and prepositions.
  • She showed memorisation of vocabulary and could recall parts of the story, though she occasionally needed reminders for correct structures.
  • The group, including Camille, performed well on the worksheet activity about Oliver and Baby’s abilities.
  • She enjoyed activities like "A present for me," Family Bingo, and the Dice Game, showing enthusiasm for interactive tasks.
  • Camille is still developing the ability to ask questions about a person's physical appearance before guessing who it is, which remains a work in progress.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

  • Camille’s behaviour was generally positive, with moments of attentiveness and enjoyment during storytime and activities.
  • She was playful with materials but remained engaged overall.
  • Camille sometimes needed gentle reminders to return materials promptly, though she did so playfully.
  • She interacted well with peers and participated in group activities, contributing to a positive classroom environment.

Final comment

____________________________________________________________

Camille has shown enthusiasm and attentiveness during class activities, particularly enjoying interactive games and story-based tasks. While she occasionally needs reminders for structure and material handling, her willingness to participate and her growing confidence are commendable. Continued practice with vocabulary and sentence structures will help her further develop her skills. Well done, Camille!
